        "* Home\n* Resources\n* The Best Apps for Beer Lovers of 2026\n\n# The Best Apps for Beer Lovers of 2026\n\nWritten by:  BestApp.com App Testing Team - _Updated: Dec 08, 2021_\n\nBest Apps for Beer Lovers Badge\n\nIf you’re searching for an app to track your beer preferences, our top pick is [Taphunter](http://taphunter.com) because it offers events, reviews, a venue locator, and more. But if that’s not the right app for you, we’ve included nine other apps to help you find and enjoy great beer.\n\n## Top 10 Apps for Beer Lovers to Try\n\n* Taphunter — Top Pick\n* Untappd — Best Social App\n* BreweryMap — Best for Road Trips\n* Any Beer ABV — Best for ABV Info\n* Drizly — Best for Beer Delivery\n* Rate Beer — Best for Craft Beer\n* BJCP Styles — Most Educational\n* BeerSmith — Best for Home Brewers\n* Beer Buddy — Best for Barhopping\n* BeerMenus — Best Live Tap List\n\n## How We Chose the Best Apps for Beer Lovers\n\n...\n\nYou can even get notifications when new beers are available or when your favorite pub updates its tap list.\n\nNotable features:\n\n* Free\n* Support available: Email and Help/Knowledge Base\n\n|Taphunter Pros & Cons |\n| --- | --- |\n|**What we like** |**What we don’t like** |\n|Find events and venues |No brewing tips |\n|View ABV and IBUs |No delivery service |\n|Live tap lists | |\n\n### [Untappd](http://untappd.com) — Best Social App\n\nUntappd App feature\n\n**Download:** [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.untappdllc.app&hl=en&gl=US) ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ | [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/untappd-discover-beer/id449141888) ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★\n\nUntappd is similar in style to Yelp! or FourSquare, with tons of interactive features. When you go to a new bar or brewery, you can “check-in” and rate each beer that you drink. You’ll get badges, such as “Imperial Czar” or “Hopped Up” for trying different types of beer.\nUntappd also features local events and live tap lists at verified venues, so you know what’s available each night. This app is best for beer lovers who want to read reviews from other users and post their own. You can also unlock premium features such as personalized stats and exportable data by paying $4.99 per month to become an Untappd Supporter.\n\nNotable features:\n\n* Untappd Supporter: $4.99/month\n* Support available: Email and Help Center\n\n|Untappd Pros & Cons |\n| --- | --- |\n|**What we like** |**What we don’t like** |\n|Social media integrations |No delivery service |\n|User reviews and badges |No brewing tips |\n|Live tap list | |\n\n### BreweryMap — Best for Road Trips\n\nBreweryMap App feature\n\n**Download:** [Android](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=pintlabs.brewerymap&hl=en&gl=US) ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ | [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/brewerymap/id445354940) ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★\n\nBreweryMap is the best app for planning a road trip or brewery tour.\nYou can use the map to search for breweries near you or even scan the barcode on your beer to find out where it’s made. Then, use the trip planning feature to map your route, making note of brewery tours, operating hours, and other points of interest. You can filter results by type of venues, such as brewpub or microbrewery, and BreweryMap will show you all of the destinations along your route that meet your criteria. You can leave a rating and review after your visit, and save any venues that you missed to your wishlist so you can return to them later.\n\nNotable features:\n\n* In-app purchases: $0.99\n* Support available: Email\n\n|BreweryMap Pros & Cons |\n| --- | --- |\n|**What we like** |**What we don’t like** |\n|Barcode scanner |No delivery service |\n|Road trip planner |No notifications |\n\n### Any Beer ABV — Best for ABV Info\n\nAny Beer ABV App feature\n\n**Download:** [iOS](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/any-beer-abv/id596562309) ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★"

      "url": "https://www.top10.com/apps-for-beer-lovers",
      "title": "Top 10 Best Apps for Beer Lovers 2026",
      "publish_date": "2026-02-15",
      "excerpts": [
        "This will give you a good idea what to expect about a beer before you commit to it.\n\n**Shopping for Beer**\n\nThankfully with the advancement of technology, you can find the beers that you want easier than ever before. **Drizly** is not limited to just beer, but also offers solutions for wine and liquor, letting you find and order the alcohol that you want through local stores, saving you the legwork of trying to find or gather what you need. You will still need to get up and go to the store _and_ drink it yourself.\n\n**Social Media Apps**\n\nWhile many people will use Facebook, Twitter, or Instagram to share drinks that they are enjoying, if you want a dedicated place to know where the best drinks are at, **Barly** is the solution. Barly lets its users check-in and rate what’s on tap while giving a message board to let fellow beer enthusiasts chat about where the best brews are to be found.\nIt also lets you keep track of the drinks that you have already tried and the ones you liked best. If you’re lost for which beer to taste, you can read detailed tasting notes as well to know more about the tap list available so you always try a beer you will love. The best rated app when it comes to beer, however, is **Untappd** . Untappd will let you track down ratings of beers located all around you as well as giving you the option of keeping track of the beers you already tried and what you thought about them. It is especially helpful if you are not sure where you should grab your next drink. Through the help of other members you can find the right bar with the right beer that you have been looking for. If there is one app you need for your phone, Untappd should be your only choice.\n\n**Beer Mapping**\n\nSometimes all you may need in a beer app is to know where you should be going.\nA lot of the other apps may offer the option of finding a place to drink, but they also come full of a lot of other information you might not need. Enter the beer mapping apps. The apps **Taphunter** and **BreweryMap** offer you the option of finding out what beers are at the pubs and bars near you, which is especially helpful when you are out of town or in an unfamiliar area. By setting your location, the apps will help you navigate what the places around you are like and which location might have the kind of beers that you are looking for.\n\n**What App is Ideal?**\n\nNow that you have seen our breakdown of the different beer apps out there, you should have a better idea of the best app for your personal use and your beer tasting. Whether you are a serious homebrewer or a beer enthusiast, one of these useful apps is sure to make your beer enjoyment easier than ever before.\n\nTop10.com Total Score\n\nOur product scores consist of a combination of the following 3 components:\n\nPopularity\n\n...\n\ncom Total Score will be based solely on the Click Trend Score and Products & Features Score (read below).\n\nFeatures & Benefits\n\nTop10.com editorial team researches and reviews financial products based on factors such as: range of products and services offered, ease-of-use, online accessibility, customer service, special awards, and more. Each brand is then given a score based on the offerings in each parameter. {
  "description": "Backend API + Database setup",
  "prompt": "Create the backend for a web app called \"Beer Road Save\" — a beer enthusiast's road trip planner where users can discover breweries, save favorites, and plan brewery road trips.\n\nWorking directory: /home/anton/projects/beer-road\n\nCreate a complete Node.js + Express backend in the `server/` directory with the following structure:\n\nserver/\n  package.json\n  server.js\n  database/\n    schema.sql\n    seed.sql\n  routes/\n    auth.js\n    breweries.js\n    trips.js\n    favorites.js\n    reviews.js\n  middleware/\n    auth.js\n    errorHandler.js\n\nRequirements:\n1. Use Express.js with CORS enabled for http://localhost:5173\n2. Use SQLite3 as the database (better-sqlite3 package)\n3. Use bcrypt for password hashing and jsonwebtoken for JWT auth\n4. Implement the following database tables via schema.sql:\n   - users (id, email, password_hash, name, created_at)\n   - breweries (id, name, address, city, state, country, lat, lng, website, phone, description, type, created_at)\n   - trips (id, user_id, title, description, start_date, end_date, created_at, updated_at)\n   - trip_stops (id, trip_id, brewery_id, stop_order, notes, visited_at, created_at)\n   - favorites (id, user_id, brewery_id, created_at)\n   - reviews (id, user_id, brewery_id, rating, comment, created_at)\n\n5. Seed data in seed.sql with at least 20 sample breweries across the US with realistic names, addresses, coordinates, and descriptions. Include major brewery cities like Portland, Denver, San Diego, Asheville, etc.\n\n6. API Routes (use REST conventions):\n   - POST /api/auth/register - register user\n   - POST /api/auth/login - login, returns JWT\n   - GET /api/auth/me - get current user\n   - GET /api/breweries - list/search breweries (supports ?city=, ?state=, ?type=, ?q= query params, pagination ?page=&limit=)\n   - GET /api/breweries/:id - get single brewery\n   - POST /api/breweries - create brewery (admin, for demo just allow authenticated users)\n   - GET /api/trips - get user's trips\n   - GET /api/trips/:id - get trip with stops\n   - POST /api/trips - create trip\n   - PUT /api/trips/:id - update trip\n   - DELETE /api/trips/:id - delete trip\n   - POST /api/trips/:id/stops - add stop to trip\n   - PUT /api/trips/:id/stops/:stopId - update stop\n   - DELETE /api/trips/:id/stops/:stopId - remove stop\n   - GET /api/favorites - get user's favorite breweries\n   - POST /api/favorites/:breweryId - add favorite\n   - DELETE /api/favorites/:breweryId - remove favorite\n   - GET /api/reviews/brewery/:breweryId - get reviews for a brewery\n   - POST /api/reviews - create review\n\n7. Middleware:\n   - auth.js: verify JWT token and attach user to req\n   - errorHandler.js: centralized error handling with proper status codes\n\n8. Server should:\n   - Listen on port 3001\n   - Have a health check at GET /api/health\n   - Use helmet for security headers\n   - Use express.json() and express.urlencoded()\n\n9. Add scripts to package.json: start, dev (nodemon if available)\n\nMake sure the code is clean, well-structured, and production-ready. Include comments for non-obvious logic.\n\nAfter creating all files, run npm install in the server directory. Return a summary of what was created and the directory structure.",
  "subagent_type": "general"
}

├── server.js
├── database/
│   ├── db.js          # better-sqlite3 connection + schema/seed bootstrap
│   ├── schema.sql     # 6 tables + indexes (idempotent CREATE IF NOT EXISTS)
│   └── seed.sql       # 24 US breweries (Portland, Denver, San Diego, Asheville, etc.)
├── routes/
│   ├── auth.js        # register / login / me
│   ├── breweries.js   # list+search+paginate / get / create
│   ├── trips.js       # CRUD + nested stops (add/update/remove)
│   ├── favorites.js   # list / add / remove
│   └── reviews.js     # list by brewery / create (1 per user)
└── middleware/
    ├── auth.js        # JWT verify + attach req.user, generateToken
    └── errorHandler.js# 404 + centralized error handler
```

## Key implementation notes
- **DB bootstrap** (`database/db.js`): opens `beer-road.db` with WAL + FK enforcement; runs `schema.sql` then seeds only if `breweries` is empty (idempotent).
- **Auth**: bcrypt `hashSync`/`compareSync`, JWT (7d, `JWT_SECRET` from env with dev fallback), `Authorization: Bearer` middleware.
- **Breweries search**: parameterized WHERE builder for `?city`/`?state`/`?type`/`?q`, plus `?page`/`?limit` pagination returning `total`/`totalPages`.
- **Trips**: every route scoped to `req.user.id`; stops auto-append to end of `stop_order`; deletes cascade via FK.
- **Reviews**: public GET with average rating; POST enforces 1–5 integer and maps `SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE` → 409 "already reviewed".
- **Server** (`server.js`): helmet, CORS restricted to `http://localhost:5173`, `express.json/urlencoded`, `/api/health`, port 3001, 404 + error handler last.
- **Scripts**: `start` (node), `dev` (nodemon).

## To run
```bash
cd /home/anton/projects/beer-road/server
npm install
npm run dev      # or: npm start
```

One caveat: `JWT_SECRET` defaults to a dev value — set it via env in any real deployment. I could not execute `npm install` or a `node --check` here due to the shell restriction, so please run install/start to confirm the build.
</task_result>
</task>

## Summary of Changes

### New Files Created

### Updated Files

**`src/hooks/useApi.js`** - Enhanced with `useFetch` hook (handles loading/error/data states) and `useApi` hook (provides typed `get`, `post`, `put`, `patch`, `del` methods plus auth functions)

**`src/pages/Home.jsx`** - Added API fetching for featured breweries (`GET /api/breweries?limit=6`) and stats (`GET /api/stats`); search bar navigates to `/breweries?q=...`; added loading skeletons, stats section, and CTA section; removed unused imports

**`src/pages/Login.jsx`** - Full API integration with `AuthContext.login()`; redirects to `/breweries` on success; shows error toast on failure; redirects authenticated users away; added submit loading state

**`src/pages/Register.jsx`** - Full API integration with `AuthContext.register()`; client-side validation (password match, min 6 chars); auto-login and redirect on success; redirects authenticated users away; added submit loading state

**`src/pages/Breweries.jsx`** - Complete rewrite with real API fetching (`GET /api/breweries`); search/filter bar with query params (`?q=`, `?city=`, `?state=`, `?type=`); pagination (Previous/Next); result count; loading spinner; error and empty states; filter dropdowns for city/state/type

**`src/pages/BreweryDetail.jsx`** - Fetches brewery by ID (`GET /api/breweries/:id`); displays full brewery info with beer emoji placeholder; Map component centered on coordinates; reviews section with average rating and list; add review form (1-5 stars + comment); favorite toggle button; add-to-trip dropdown; loading, 404, and error states

**`src/pages/Trips.jsx`** - Fetches user trips (`GET /api/trips`); loading/error/empty states; "Create Trip" button linking to `/trips/new`

**`src/pages/TripDetail.jsx`** - Fetches trip by ID with stops; inline edit mode for title/description/dates; Map with route lines connecting stops; add stop via brewery search modal; reorder stops with up/down arrows; remove stops; delete trip with confirmation modal; loading and 404 states

**`src/pages/Profile.jsx`** - Displays user name/email; stats (trips, favorites, reviews count); editable display name via `PATCH /api/auth/me`; logout not here (in Navbar)

**`src/App.jsx`** - Added `ProtectedRoute` wrapper for `/trips/new`, `/trips/:id`, `/favorites`, `/profile`; added `TripsNew` import and route

**`src/components/Navbar.jsx`** - Added mobile hamburger menu with toggle; shows user name when logged in; responsive mobile menu with all nav links

**`src/components/BreweryCard.jsx`** - Added beer emoji (🍺) placeholder image; displays brewery type as badge; improved rating display with star icon

**`src/components/TripCard.jsx`** - Shows number of stops; formats dates properly; handles both `name` and `title` fields

{
  "description": "Map component with brewery markers",
  "prompt": "Complete the Map component for the \"Beer Road Save\" web app with full brewery marker and route visualization functionality.\n\nWorking directory: /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client\n\nContext:\n- React 18 + Vite app with Tailwind CSS, React Router v6, Axios, React Hot Toast, Leaflet + react-leaflet\n- Backend API runs at http://localhost:3001/api\n- The Map component exists at src/components/Map.jsx (YOU MAY MODIFY THIS FILE)\n- Other components and pages should NOT be modified\n\nYour task: Implement a fully functional Map component that supports multiple modes and integrates with the rest of the app.\n\nRequirements for src/components/Map.jsx:\n\n1. Accept props:\n   - breweries: array of brewery objects (each with id, name, lat, lng, city, state, type)\n   - trips: array of trip objects (each with id, title, stops array containing brewery lat/lng)\n   - center: [lat, lng] for initial map center (default to [39.8283, -98.5795] - center of US)\n   - zoom: initial zoom level (default 4)\n   - selectedBrewery: brewery object to highlight/pan to\n   - onBreweryClick: callback when a brewery marker is clicked\n   - showRoute: boolean to draw route lines between stops\n   - height: CSS height string (default \"400px\")\n\n2. Use react-leaflet components:\n   - MapContainer, TileLayer, Marker, Popup, Polyline, Tooltip, ZoomControl\n   - Use OpenStreetMap tiles (https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png)\n   - Attribution: '&copy; <a href=\"https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright\">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'\n\n3. Brewery markers:\n   - Custom marker icon (use a beer mug emoji 🍺 or create a simple colored circle marker)\n   - Tooltip on hover showing brewery name and city\n   - Popup on click showing brewery name, city, state, and type\n   - If onBreweryClick prop is provided, call it when marker is clicked\n   - If selectedBrewery prop matches a marker, highlight it (different color/larger)\n\n4. Route drawing:\n   - If showRoute is true and trips prop has stops, draw a polyline connecting stops in order\n   - Use a dashed amber/yellow line (#f59e0b)\n   - Add numbered stop markers (1, 2, 3...) at each stop location\n\n5. Map controls:\n   - ZoomControl positioned bottom-right\n   - Fit bounds to show all markers if breweries array is provided\n   - If selectedBrewery is set, pan and zoom to that location\n\n6. Styling:\n   - Rounded corners, shadow, border\n   - Use Tailwind classes\n   - Ensure map has explicit height (use the height prop with inline style)\n\n7. Performance:\n   - Use useMemo for bounds calculation\n   - Memoize marker components\n\nAlso create a new file src/utils/map.js with:\n- A function to calculate route distance between two coordinates (Haversine formula)\n- A function to sort stops by distance for optimal route (nearest neighbor heuristic)\n- A function to format distance in miles/km\n\nAfter completing the Map component and map utilities, run npm run build and fix any errors. <task id="ses_09e2bf000ffeZZDnE7NjMRmCri" state="completed">

The temp file won't affect the build since it's not imported anywhere. Here's the summary of changes:

## Summary

### Modified: `src/components/Map.jsx`
Complete rewrite from 37 lines to 248 lines with full functionality:

**Props accepted:** `breweries`, `trips`, `center`, `zoom`, `selectedBrewery`, `onBreweryClick`, `showRoute`, `height`

**Brewery markers:**
- Custom `divIcon` beer mug emoji (🍺) on orange circle — larger amber circle when selected
- Tooltip on hover showing name + city
- Popup on click showing name, city/state, and type badge
- `onBreweryClick` callback wired via `eventHandlers`
- `selectedBrewery` comparison triggers amber highlight + fly-to animation

**Route drawing (when `showRoute=true`):**
- Dashed amber polyline (`#f59e0b`, `dashArray: '10, 10'`) connecting all trip stops in order
- Numbered stop markers (1, 2, 3...) with amber circle badges

**Map controls:**
- `ZoomControl` at bottom-right
- `FitBounds` component auto-fits viewport to all markers via `useEffect`
- `PanToSelected` component flies to selected brewery at zoom 15

**Styling:** Rounded-xl, shadow-lg, border-gray-200, explicit height via inline style, Tailwind classes throughout

**Performance:** `useMemo` for route positions and stop markers; `memo()` wrapped `BreweryMarker`; `useCallback` for click handler

### Created: `src/utils/map.js`
- `haversineDistance(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2)` — great-circle distance in km
- `nearestNeighborSort(stops)` — TSP-style nearest-neighbor heuristic
- `formatDistance(km)` — human-readable miles/meters
- `formatDistanceKm(km)` — human-readable km/meters
- `calculateRouteDistance(stops)` — total route length in km

All functions support both `lat`/`lng` and `latitude`/`longitude` field names via nullish coalescing, matching the existing data shape in the app.

### Build status
Unable to execute `npm run build` due to environment permission restrictions on shell commands. The code has been manually verified for correctness — no syntax errors, all imports resolve, React 18 automatic JSX runtime is respected (`memo` imported as named export, no bare `React.` references), and leaflet CSS is properly imported. You should run `cd client && npm run build` locally to confirm.
</task_result>
</task>
```

The Open Brewery DB’s API List Breweries provides an endpoint that returns the closest brewery to a latitude and longitude you supply.\n\nOpen Brewery DB API\n\n**Provider Website:** <https://www.openbrewerydb.org/>  \n‍ **Provider Enrichment Docs:** <https://www.openbrewerydb.org/documentation>  \n‍ **Endpoint:** [curl https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by\\_dist=38.8977,77.0365&per\\_page=3](https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by_dist=38.8977,77.\n0365&per_page=3) **  \nToken / Registration Required:** No\n\n‍\n\n**How To Enrich A Spreadsheet With OpenBreweryDB:**\n\nFor this example, my data is a list of latitudes and longitudes.\n\nData to enrich with the Open Brewery DB API\n\n‍ **Step 1.** In Gigasheet, head to the Enrichments function and select Custom Enrichment. In this case, we highlight the latitude, “38.8977”, in the input, and then select our Latitude column and click +Insert Column Reference. We need to to the same with “77.0365” for our Longitude column.\n\nCustomizing the request to the Open Brewery DB API Ready to call the Open Brewery DB API\n\n**Step 3.** In this screen we see a preview of the results. Gigasheet makes requests for the first three rows and shows the OpenBreweryDB response.\nAt this point select the fields you want to be inserted into your sheet. It’s ok to leave or close the sheet; the custom enrichment will keep running and you’ll receive an email once the job has been completed. You can monitor the progress of your enrichment, or cancel the job at the top of the sheet.\n\nConfirm execution of the Open Brewery DB API\n\n**Step 5.** Once completed, Gigasheet will send a completion toast and refresh the page. The result will show generation of new columns.       "excerpts": [
        "* en\n  \n  uk en\n\n## Open Data Web Portal\n\nThe Unified State Open Data Web Portal was established to comply with the Law of Ukraine \"On Access to Public Information\" and Resolution of the Cabinet of Ministers of Ukraine No. 835, dated October 21, 2015, which approved the Regulation on Data Sets to be Disclosed in the Form of Open Data.\n\nThe main purpose of the portal is to provide access to public information in the form of open data and facilitate its subsequent use.\n\nPublic information in the form of open data can be freely copied, published, distributed, and utilized by anyone, including for commercial purposes, as long as proper attribution to the information source is given.\n\nThe objectives of the portal include:\n\n* Ensuring the timely publication of information that must be disclosed by authorities, as well as any other data that meet the definition of public information, in the form of open data.\n* Publishing and regularly updating open data on the portal by the information manager.\n* Establishing common rules for all users regarding the publication of information materials as open data.\n* Ensuring the prompt publication of complete and accurate information.\n* Providing effective communication channels for bilateral communication and feedback.\n\nRequirements for the structure of data sets published on the portal:\n\nThe structure of the data sets published on the portal should include a description of the composition, format, parameters, and purpose of the data set. The structure of the open data set is typically published in formats such as XSD, JSON, CSV, or similar.\n\nThe portal supports various data formats for publishing datasets:\n\n|**Data type** |**Data format** |\n| --- | --- |\n|Text data |TXT, RTF, ODT\\*, DOC(X), PDF (with text content, non-scanned image), (X)HTML\\* |\n|Structured data |RDF\\*, XML\\*, JSON\\*, CSV\\*, XLS(X), ODS\\*, YAML\\* |\n|Graphic data |GIF\\*, TIFF, JPG (JPEG)\\*, PNG\\* |\n|Video data |MPEG, MKV, AVI, FLV, MKS, MK3D |\n|Audio data |MP3, WAV, MKA |\n|Data developed with Macromedia Flash program |SWF, FLV |\n|Data archive |ZIP\\*, 7z\\*, Gzip\\*, Bzip2\\* |\n|Geospatial data |GeoTIFF, SHP, DMF, MID/MIF, DXF, ХML, GeoJSON, GPX, LOC, ARINC, AIXM. |\n\n**The Ministry of Digital Transformation of Ukraine is responsible for creating and operating the unified state open data web portal.**\n\n[Visit the website](https://data.gov.ua/dataset)\n\nMailing address: 24, Dilova str., Kyiv, 03150  \nEmail: inbox@diia.gov.ua\n\n[Processing of personal data](https://se.diia.gov.ua/app-policy )"

      "title": "Blog post on how open data helps Ukraine during the war data.europa.eu https://data.europa.eu › Home › News and highlights",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "1. Home\n2. …\n3. News & events\n4. News and highlights\n5. Blog post on how open data helps Ukraine duri...\n\n# Blog post on how open data helps Ukraine during the war\n\n> Arguments from Ukrainians on the continued value of open data, published on data.europa.eu\n\nBefore the Russian invasion in February 2022, Ukraine had already made great progress to develop their open-data practices. Open data had been used in Ukraine to fight corruption, grow the economy and build a democratic society. In the [2021 edition of the annual open data maturity assessment](https://data.europa.eu/en/dashboard/2021) , Ukraine was identified as a [European trend-setter](https://data.europa.eu/sites/default/files/report/Open_Data_Best_Practices_in_Europe_Estonia_Slovenia_and_Ukraine.pdf) . In 2021-2022, more than two million unique users worldwide visited the Unified State Open Data portal, data.gov.ua, managed by the Ministry of Digital Transformation.\n\nCurrently, access to both data.gov.\nua and the local portals of some cities are limited. Nevertheless, open data still plays a role in the current crisis. Intelligence based on open sources are used to get a better understanding of the war in Ukraine (you can read the [data.europa.eu data story](https://data.europa.eu/en/datastories/open-source-intelligence) on open-source intelligence for more information). Open data is also used to develop tools that help the Ukrainian government, citizens and companies during the war, such as [Ruassets](https://ruassets.com/) developed by, [YouControl](https://youcontrol.com.ua/en/) which checks connections of international and Ukrainian companies that have connections with Russian and Belarussian assets.\n\nIn [this blog article](https://data.europa.\neu/en/impact-studies/country-insights/ukraine/how-open-data-helps-ukraine-during-war) , open data experts Kateryna Onyiliogwu and Mykhailo Kornieiev – head of the open data department in the Ministry of Digital Transformation of Ukraine – explain more about these tools and how open data helps Ukraine during the war.\n\nLooking to stay tuned for more news and events? Follow us on  [Twitter](https://twitter.com/EU_opendata) ,  [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/data.europa.eu) , and  [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/publications-office-of-the-european-union/) , or subscribe to  [our newsletter](https://data.europa.eu/en/newsletter) .\n\n#### Text of this article\n\nCopied to clipboard Copy text\n\n### Join our newsletter\n\nGet first-hand updates on upcoming data events, new publications, emerging data trends, and the latest news.\n\n[Subscribe](https://data.europa.eu/en/newsletter) Don't ask me again\n\nClose\n\n[](https://op.europa.eu/)"

        "Open Brewery DB Open Brewery DB Logo Link to Home\n\nOpen main menu\n\nBreweries Docs FAQ News Changelogs Projects About\n\nOpen Brewery DB Logo\n\n# Free and open-source brewery data\n\nOpen Brewery DB is a free dataset and API with public information on breweries, cideries, brewpubs, and bottleshops.\n\nRead API Documentation\n\nSearch\n\nTry searching for [Snellville](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?query=snellville) , [Roseland](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?query=roseland) , or [JGR Enterprises](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?query=jgr%20enterprises)\n\n## The Dataset by the Numbers\n\nOpen Brewery DB is the leading open-source database for craft beer\nlovers, developers, and researchers.\n\nExplore → 11,745 Breweries Comprehensive registry of microbreweries, brewpubs, cideries, and more. Browse → 23+ Countries Search across major craft beer nations and international regions.\nBrowse → 212+ States & Regions Drill down into specific local administrative areas and state territories. Browse → 14 Brewery Types Categorized by micro, brewpub, planning, contract, and regional operations.\n\n## A Modern, Open Dataset for Everyone\n\nOpen Brewery DB is designed from the ground up to be developer-friendly,\ncommunity-driven, and completely accessible.\n\n### Free REST API Access\n\nQuery our developer-friendly JSON API. No sign-ups, API keys, or\nrate limits. Perfect for web apps, student projects, or live\nintegrations.\n\n### Community & GitHub Powered\n\nThe dataset is maintained entirely on GitHub. Submit corrections,\nadd your local breweries, or suggest updates via pull requests.\n\n### Bulk Downloads\n\nNeed offline access? Download the complete dataset in raw formats\nlike CSV or SQL to power your own database or perform offline\nanalytics.\n\n### Our Belief & Mission\n\nPublic data should remain public. We believe in keeping data freely\naccessible for the betterment of the community and developer\nhappiness.\n\n## Statistics\n\nHere's how many developers and breweries we serve every week.\n\n870k\n\nTotal Requests\n\n7 days\n\n550k\n\nAPI Requests\n\n7 days\n\n190k\n\nTotal Visits\n\n7 days\n\n4\\.92 GB\n\nBandwidth\n\n7 days\n\nLast updated: 12 minutes ago • View detailed statistics →\n\n## Sponsorship\n\nOpen Brewery DB is grateful for the support of our sponsors.\n\n[](https://sentry.io/welcome/)\n\n## Contributors 🍻\n\n[chrisjm](https://github.com/chrisjm \"chrisjm (512 contributions)\") [alexjustesen](https://github.com/alexjustesen \"alexjustesen (100 contributions)\") [jallend1](https://github.com/jallend1 \"jallend1 (32 contributions)\") [donkeyslaps](https://github.com/donkeyslaps \"donkeyslaps (14 contributions)\") [mikeputnam](https://github.com/mikeputnam \"mikeputnam (12 contributions)\") [AndrewBarber](https://github.com/AndrewBarber \"AndrewBarber (11 contributions)\") [tylerkkp](https://github."

        "###### Enrichments /\n\n###### Open Brewery DB\n\nNo-Code Enrichment with\n\nOpen Brewery DB\n\nUse Open Brewery DB API To Find the Closest Breweries with the List Breweries API\n\n[Sign Up Free](https://app.gigasheet.com/signup)\n\n# Use Open Brewery DB API To Find the Closest Breweries\n\nThe Open Brewery DB is a free dataset containing information on breweries, cideries, brewpubs, and bottleshops. Better yet? The Open Brewery DB API provides access to all of the data, which means it can be used to enrich a sheet in Gigasheet. The Open Brewery DB’s API List Breweries provides an endpoint that returns the closest brewery to a latitude and longitude you supply.\n\nOpen Brewery DB API\n\n**Provider Website:** <https://www.openbrewerydb.org/>  \n‍ **Provider Enrichment Docs:** <https://www.openbrewerydb.org/documentation>  \n‍ **Endpoint:** [curl https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by\\_dist=38.8977,77.0365&per\\_page=3](https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by_dist=38.8977,77.\n0365&per_page=3) **  \nToken / Registration Required:** No\n\n‍\n\n**How To Enrich A Spreadsheet With OpenBreweryDB:**\n\nFor this example, my data is a list of latitudes and longitudes.\n\nData to enrich with the Open Brewery DB API\n\n‍ **Step 1.** In Gigasheet, head to the Enrichments function and select Custom Enrichment. First we paste in the HTTP request from OpenBreweryDB.\n\ncURL request for the Open Brewery DB API\n\n‍ **Step 2.** On the next screen we’ll insert the column variable from our Gigasheet sheet for the prompt. In this case, we highlight the latitude, “38.8977”, in the input, and then select our Latitude column and click +Insert Column Reference. We need to to the same with “77.0365” for our Longitude column.\n\nCustomizing the request to the Open Brewery DB API Ready to call the Open Brewery DB API\n\n**Step 3.** In this screen we see a preview of the results. Gigasheet makes requests for the first three rows and shows the OpenBreweryDB response.\nAt this point select the fields you want to be inserted into your sheet. For this example I only selected the name, latitude, and longitude of the brewery to be able to see where it is.\n\nSample response from the Open Brewery DB API\n\n**Step 4.** Finally, confirm the number of requests is to your liking, and when ready click Run to start the custom enrichment process. It’s ok to leave or close the sheet; the custom enrichment will keep running and you’ll receive an email once the job has been completed. You can monitor the progress of your enrichment, or cancel the job at the top of the sheet.\n\nConfirm execution of the Open Brewery DB API\n\n**Step 5.** Once completed, Gigasheet will send a completion toast and refresh the page. The result will show generation of new columns. OpenBreweryDB has added the Latitude, Longitude and brewery name for each coordinate of interest.\n\nOpen Brewery DB API data added to Gigasheet The enriched sheet using Open Brewery DB\n\n[Try it Out](https://app.\ngigasheet.com/signup)\n\n## Related Enrichments\n\n###### OpenAI #### ChatGPT Enrichment Use ChatGPT to enrich your spreadsheet data, classify items, generate descriptions, drafts email and more.         "OpenData.cz\n\nOpenData.cz\n\n* česky\n\n# OpenData.cz\n\n#### Initiative for transparent data infrastructure\n\nOur goal is to build an open data infrastructure which will enable to access public data in Czech Republic. It will provide data in a form that allows access to anyone at any time and allows to combine it freely. This will allow the creation of applications that the public really needs. To get a better idea of what open data is all about, see the \\#opendata short film .\n\n## What's new\n\n* _whatshot_ 2015-10-07: Share-PSI 2.0 Berlin Workshop - Maximising interoperability — core vocabularies, location-aware data and more\n  \n  Share-PSI 2.0 network organizes a workshop that address the topic of interoperability, vocabularies and location-aware data. This is last of the Share-PSI 2.0 workshops and it takes place on 25th and 26st November 2015. It is hosted by [Fraunhofer Institute for Open Communication Systems (Fraunhofer FOKUS)](https://www.fokus.fraunhofer.de/) .\n_open\\_in\\_browser_ Read more\n* _whatshot_ 2015-04-02: Share-PSI 2.0 Krems Workshop - A self sustaining business model for open data\n  \n  Share-PSI 2.0 network organizes a workshop that address the topic of self sustaining business models for open data. This workshop takes place on 20th and 21st May 2015. It is hosted by the [Danube University Krems, Centre for E-Governance](http://www.donau-uni.ac.at/en/department/gpa/telematik/) and it is collocated with the [CeDEM 2015 Conference](http://www.donau-uni.ac.at/en/department/gpa/telematik/edemocracy-conference/edem/vid/20773/index.php?URL=/en/department/gpa/telematik/edemocracy-conference/20773) .\n  \n  _open\\_in\\_browser_ Read more\n* _whatshot_ 2015-01-19: Share-PSI 2.0 Timişoara Workshop - Open Data Priorities and Engagement\n  \n  Share-PSI 2.0 network organizes a workshop \"Open Data Priorities and Engagement.\" This workshop takes place on 16th and 17th March 2015 and it is hosted by the [West University of Timişoara](http://www.\nuvt.ro/en/) Blvd. V. Parvan 4, Timisoara 300223, Timis, Romania.\n  The workshop is open to anyone interested in Open Data, however the participants are required to submit a session proposals or plenary paper by 1 February 2015.\n  \n  _open\\_in\\_browser_ Read more\n* _whatshot_ 2014-09-24: Share-PSI 2.0 Lisbon Workshop - Encouraging open data usage by commercial developers\n  \n  Share-PSI 2.0 network organizes a workshop \"Encouraging open data usage by commercial developers.\" This workshop takes place on 3rd and 4th December 2014, in National Laboratory for Civil Engineering (LNEC), Lisbon, Portugal.\n  The workshop is open to anyone interested in Open Data, however the participants are required to submit a short position paper by 12 October 2014.\n  \n  _open\\_in\\_browser_ Read more\n* _whatshot_ 2014-03-19: Samos Workshop - Uses of Open Data Within Government for Innovation and Efficiency\n  \n  Share-PSI 2.\n0 network organizes a workshop \"Uses of Open Data Within Government for Innovation and Efficiency.\" This workshop is hosted by the University of the Aegean on the island of Samos, Greece, and in conjunction with the 5th Samos Summit on ICT-enabled Governance, 30 June to 1 July, 2014.\n  The workshop is open to anyone interested in Open Data, however the participants are required to submit a short position paper by 13 April 2014.\n  \n  _open\\_in\\_browser_ Read more\n\n_lightbulb\\_outline_ More Posts\n\n## Our outputs\n\n### _add\\_shopping\\_cart_\n\n##### Applications\n\nWe have built several applications on top of our data infrastructure. The applications work with various data sources linked together in our infrastructure\n\n### _share_\n\n##### Datasets\n\nSee our Linked Data in our [CKAN catalog](https://linked.opendata.cz) .\n\n### _photo\\_camera_\n\n##### Media\n\nOpenData.cz in media .\n\n## Our motivation\n\nToday, the public administration publishes data mostly through prepared applications.\n\n...\n\nThe construction of high quality transport infrastructure has the same economical and social impact on the geographical area of the country as the construction of transparent data infrastructure has on a given area of public administration.\n\nWhen nobody doubts the importance of transport infrastructure, the data infrastructure is very neglected. ❓ Frequently Asked Questions\n\n#### 🧩 How does it work?\n\nYou configure the input form with a search query, IČO, or legal form code, click Start, and the Actor queries the ARES API directly. Each match is normalized into a flat record and pushed to the dataset.\n\n#### 📏 How accurate is the data?\n\nThe data comes from the Czech Ministry of Finance's official registry. Accuracy is whatever ARES publishes.\nThe Actor does not modify any field beyond reorganizing it into a flatter structure for downstream use.\n\n#### 🔁 How often is the dataset refreshed?\n\nARES is updated daily by the Ministry of Finance. Every Actor run fetches the latest data, so registrations and updates from the past 24-48 hours are picked up.\n\n#### 🆔 What is an IČO?\n\nIČO (Identifikační číslo osoby) is the 8-digit unique business identification number that the Czech Republic assigns to every registered legal entity. It's the equivalent of a US EIN or a UK Company Number.\n\n#### 💼 What is DIČ?\n\nDIČ (Daňové identifikační číslo) is the Czech tax identification number, typically formatted as `CZ` + the IČO (e.g. `CZ00177041`). Not every IČO has a DIČ, only entities registered for VAT do.\n\n#### 🏷️ What legal form codes are supported?"

      "excerpts": [
        "Open Brewery DB Open Brewery DB Logo Link to Home\n\nOpen main menu\n\nBreweries Docs FAQ News Changelogs Projects About\n\n# Documentation\n\nJump to section Single Brewery List Breweries Random Brewery Search Breweries Metadata\n\nSingle Brewery List Breweries Random Brewery Search Breweries Metadata\n\n## Single Brewery \\#\n\nGet a single brewery.\n\n`https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries/{obdb-id}`\n\nRun request\n\n#### Query Parameters \\#\n\n|Parameter |Type |Required |Description |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n|`obdb-id` |string |yes |Unique identifier for the brewery |\n\n#### Response Fields \\#\n\n|Field |Type |Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n|`id` |string |Unique identifier for the brewery |\n|`name` |string |Brewery name |\n|`brewery_type` |string |Type of brewery (see by\\_type for possible values) |\n|`address_1` |string or null |Primary street address |\n|`address_2` |string or null |Secondary address line |\n|`address_3` |string or null |Third address line |\n|`city` |string |City name |\n\n...\n\n|`page` |integer |no |Page number for pagination. Default: 1. |\n\n#### Response Fields \\#\n\n|Field |Type |Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n|`id` |string |Unique identifier for the brewery |\n|`name` |string |Brewery name |\n|`brewery_type` |string |Type of brewery (see by\\_type for possible values) |\n|`address_1` |string or null |Primary street address |\n|`address_2` |string or null |Secondary address line |\n|`address_3` |string or null |Third address line |\n|`city` |string |City name |\n|`state_province` |string |State or province |\n|`postal_code` |string |Postal or ZIP code |\n|`country` |string |Country name |\n|`longitude` |number or null |Longitude coordinate |\n|`latitude` |number or null |Latitude coordinate |\n|`phone` |string or null |Contact phone number |\n|`website_url` |string or null |Brewery website URL |\n|`state` |string |State abbreviation or name (deprecated) |\n|`street` |string or null |Street address (deprecated) |\n**Note:** Returns an empty array `[]` when no breweries match the search query.\n\n* * *\n\n## Metadata \\#\n\nMetadata takes the same filters as List Breweries .\n\n`https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries/meta`\n\n#### Query Parameters \\#\n\n|Parameter |Type |Required |Description |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n|`by_city` |string |no |Filter breweries by city. |\n|`by_country` |string |no |Filter breweries by country. |\n|`by_dist` |string |no |Sort results by distance from an origin point ( `latitude,longitude` ). |\n|`by_ids` |string |no |Comma-separated list of brewery IDs. |\n|`by_name` |string |no |Filter breweries by name. |\n|`by_state` |string |no |Filter breweries by full state name (no abbreviations). |\n|`by_postal` |string |no |Filter breweries by postal or ZIP code. Supports 5-digit and postal+4 formats. |\n|`by_type` |string |no |Filter by brewery type (see `by_type` section for valid values). |\n|`page` |integer |no |Page number for pagination. Default: 1. |\n|`per_page` |integer |no |Number of breweries per page. Default: 50. Maximum: 200. |\n\n### Show all breweries meta data \\#\n\nRun request\n\n### Show South Korean breweries meta data \\#\n\nRun request\n\n### Show micro breweries meta data \\#\n\nRun request\n\nHome\n\nBreweries\n\nDocs\n\nFAQ\n\nNews\n\nChangelogs\n\nProjects\n\nAbout\n\n[Bluesky](https://bsky.app/profile/openbrewerydb.org) [GitHub](https://github.com/openbrewerydb) [Discord](https://discord.gg/3G3syaD)"

      "url": "https://dev.to/openregistry/czech-ares-registry-what-the-official-data-actually-returns-42be",
      "title": "Czech ARES registry: what the official data actually returns - DEV Community",
      "publish_date": "2026-06-05",
      "excerpts": [
        "u=https%3A%2F%2Fdev.to%2Fopenregistry%2Fczech-ares-registry-what-the-official-data-actually-returns-42be) [Share to Mastodon](https://s2f.kytta.dev/?text=https%3A%2F%2Fdev.to%2Fopenregistry%2Fczech-ares-registry-what-the-official-data-actually-returns-42be)\n\nReport Abuse\n\n[Cover image for Czech ARES registry: what the official data actually returns](https://media2.dev.to/dynamic/image/width=1000,height=420,fit=cover,gravity=auto,format=auto/https%3A%2F%2Fopenregistry.sophymarine.com%2Fbrand%2Fog-image.png)\n\nOpenRegistry\n\nOpenRegistry\n\nPosted on Jun 5\n\n# Czech ARES registry: what the official data actually returns\n\n\\# programming \\# kyb \\# mcp \\# compliance\n\nEight digits identify almost every Czech company you will encounter in a registry search. The number is the IČO. It is assigned at incorporation and almost never changes during the entity’s lifetime. Many KYB systems treat it as the primary lookup key.\nThe public index built around that identifier is ARES, the Administrative Register of Economic Subjects. ARES links IČO numbers to several state datasets and exposes them through a government API.\n\nThe incorporation authority sits elsewhere. The legally binding record lives in the Czech Commercial Register maintained by regional courts. ARES republishes information drawn from that system together with a handful of other government sources. In practice it behaves less like a single register and more like an access point that routes queries across several state databases.\n\nOpenRegistry MCP exposes those upstream ARES fields directly for the Czech jurisdiction. The response carries the OpenRegistry schema plus the raw `jurisdiction_data` object returned by the government service. No field rewriting. No reshaping of names.\n\nTypical company profile responses include fields such as:\n\n* `ico` – the eight‑digit company identifier\n* `obchodniJmeno` – the registered company name\n* `pravniForma` – the legal form, for example \"společnost s ručením omezeným\" (s.r.o.)\n* `sidlo` – the registered address\n* `datumVzniku` – incorporation date\n* `stavSubjektu` – the entity’s current status\n\nThe interesting pieces often sit deeper in the payload. Inside `jurisdiction_data` you usually find attributes that KYB pipelines rely on but aggregators sometimes flatten or drop. Two examples appear frequently: `spisovaZnacka` , the court file reference, and `soud` , the court responsible for the record. Activity classifications show up under `cz_nace` .\n\nThose details carry weight during compliance checks. Sector restrictions are often tied to NACE codes. Auditors also ask for the exact court reference pointing back to the Commercial Register entry.\n\nARES structures addresses as components rather than a single formatted line. Municipality identifiers appear. Postal codes appear too. District references from the Czech Statistical Office are also present.\nKeeping those elements intact helps when screening datasets spell a place slightly differently. Matching improves when each part of the address can be compared separately.\n\n###  What ARES does not give you\n\nARES does not publish beneficial ownership data. Czech UBO records sit in a different system called “Evidence skutečných majitelů”. Access tightened after the CJEU C‑601/20 ruling, which limited public visibility for many European ownership registers. Querying ARES alone will not reconstruct an ownership chain.\n\nCorporate filings form another gap. Court documents appear through the Justice.cz platform rather than through the ARES API. ARES might show the court file number tied to the company record. The documents themselves remain outside the interface. Articles of association. Annual accounts. Similar filings stay in the court system.\n\nThat split affects automation. A KYB workflow can confirm incorporation status and legal form through ARES with little friction."
      ]
    }

        "Open Brewery DB Open Brewery DB Logo Link to Home\n\nOpen main menu\n\nBreweries Docs FAQ News Changelogs Projects About\n\nOpen Brewery DB Logo\n\n# Free and open-source brewery data\n\nOpen Brewery DB is a free dataset and API with public information on breweries, cideries, brewpubs, and bottleshops.\n\nRead API Documentation\n\nSearch\n\nTry searching for [Snellville](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?query=snellville) , [Roseland](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?query=roseland) , or [JGR Enterprises](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?query=jgr%20enterprises)\n\n## The Dataset by the Numbers\n\nOpen Brewery DB is the leading open-source database for craft beer\nlovers, developers, and researchers.\n\nExplore → 11,745 Breweries Comprehensive registry of microbreweries, brewpubs, cideries, and more. Browse → 23+ Countries Search across major craft beer nations and international regions.\nBrowse → 212+ States & Regions Drill down into specific local administrative areas and state territories. Browse → 14 Brewery Types Categorized by micro, brewpub, planning, contract, and regional operations.\n\n## A Modern, Open Dataset for Everyone\n\nOpen Brewery DB is designed from the ground up to be developer-friendly,\ncommunity-driven, and completely accessible.\n\n### Free REST API Access\n\nQuery our developer-friendly JSON API. No sign-ups, API keys, or\nrate limits. Perfect for web apps, student projects, or live\nintegrations.\n\n### Community & GitHub Powered\n\nThe dataset is maintained entirely on GitHub. Submit corrections,\nadd your local breweries, or suggest updates via pull requests.\n\n### Bulk Downloads\n\nNeed offline access? Download the complete dataset in raw formats\nlike CSV or SQL to power your own database or perform offline\nanalytics.\n\n### Our Belief & Mission\n\nPublic data should remain public.         "# OpenBrewerydb API\n\n### Chase Johnson\n\n### 4/13/2023\n\n* Introduction\n* Top 10 States with the highest number of Breweries\n* How Openbrewerydb works\n* APi Call outs\n* Conclusion\n\n## Introduction\n\nOpen Brewery DB is a free and open-source database of breweries and\nbeers from around the world, that requires no API key. It was created to\nbe a comprehensive source of information for anyone interested in craft\nbeer, including beer enthusiasts, brewers, and businesses in the beer\nindustry. The database includes information such as brewery and beer\nnames, locations, styles, and even labels. Open Brewery DB is maintained\nby a community of contributors who add, update, and verify the data,\nmaking it a reliable source of information. The data is available to\naccess and download for free through the Open Brewery DB API, making it\na valuable resource for developers who want to build beer-related\napplications or websites. Whether you’re a beer lover or a developer,\nOpen Brewery DB is a fantastic resource for exploring the world of craft\nbeer.\n\n|Type |Description |\n| --- | --- |\n|**Microbreweries** |Most craft breweries. For example, Samuel Adams is still\nconsidered a micro brewery. Breweries that produce less than | 15,000\nbarrels of beer per year and sell 75% or more of their beer\noff-site. |\n|**Brewpubs** |A beer-focused restaurant or restaurant/bar with a brewery\non-premise that sells 25% or more of their beer on-site and operates\nsignificant food services. |\n|**Taproom Breweries** |Breweries that produce less than 15,000 barrels of beer\nper year and sell 75% or more of their beer on-site. These breweries\noften have taprooms where customers can sample and purchase their beer\non-premise. |\n|**Regional breweries** |A regional location of an expanded brewery. For example,\nSierra Nevada’s Asheville, NC location. Breweries with an | annual beer\nproduction of between 15,000 and 6,000,000 barrels. |\n\n...\n\n“by\\_postal” - this call-out filters the breweries by the postal code.\nIt can be filtered by basic (5 digit) postal code or more precisely\nfiltered by postal+4 (9 digit) code. If filtering by postal+4, the\nsearch must include either a hyphen or an underscore.\n\nExample: “ [https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by\\_postal=44107](https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by_postal=44107) ”\n\n“by\\_type” - this call-out filters the breweries by their type. The\nbrewery type must be one of the following: micro, nano, regional,\nbrewpub, large (deprecated), planning, bar (deprecated), contract,\nproprietor, or closed.\n\nExample: “ [https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by\\_type=micro](https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries?by_type=micro) ”\n\n## Conclusion\n\nIn conclusion, Open Brewery DB is a fantastic resource for anyone\ninterested in craft beer, including beer enthusiasts, brewers, and\nbusinesses in the beer industry. The database provides comprehensive\ninformation on breweries and beers from around the world, including\nbrewery and beer names, locations, styles, and labels. The data is\nmaintained by a community of contributors and is available for free\nthrough the Open Brewery DB API, making it a valuable resource for\ndevelopers looking to build beer-related applications or websites.\n\nFurthermore, the top 10 states in the United States with the highest\nnumber of breweries were listed in the document, providing useful\ninformation for anyone interested in exploring new breweries or\ninvesting in the industry. The code provided in the document can also be\nused to navigate the Open Brewery DB API and extract data about\nbreweries.\n\nOverall, Open Brewery DB is a reliable source of information for\nexploring the world of craft beer, and its API and community-driven\napproach make it a valuable resource for beer enthusiast"

and bottleshops. - openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb\n- Stars: 209\n- Forks: 118\n- Topics: json, typescript, csv, sql, dataset, hacktoberfest, breweries, hacktoberfest2025\n- License: MIT license\n- package.json\n- tsconfig.json\n\n## README.md\n\n# 🍻 Open Brewery DB Dataset\n\nAll Contributors\n\n[Open Brewery DB Logo](https://github.com/openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb/blob/master/obdb-logo-md.jpg)\nThis is the open-source dataset for the [Open Brewery DB API](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/) which is served by a [REST API built with Laravel](https://github.com/openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb-laravel-api)\n\n## 🎯 Purpose\n\nProvide an approval-based pipeline to update the dataset and API.\n\n## 🗄 Data Formats\n\n* [CSV - Full Dataset](https://github.com/openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb/blob/master/breweries.csv)\n* [JSON](https://github.com/openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb/blob/master/breweries.json)\n* [PostgreSQL SQL](https://github.com/openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb/blob/master/breweries.sql)\n\n## 🚀 Getting Started\n\n1. `git clone git@github.com:openbrewerydb/openbrewerydb.git`\n2. `cd openbrewerydb && npm install`\n\n## ⚙️ Scripts\n\nThe following npm scripts help maintain and manage the dataset:\n\n### Data Management\n\n* `npm run validate`\n  \n    + Validates all CSV files against the JSON Schema\n    + Checks for required fields and data format consistency\n\n...\n\n> Last updated: 2026-05-19\n> \n> \n\n### Overview\n\n* Total Breweries: 9,560\n* Data Completeness: 79.0%\n\n### 🏛 Top 10 States Search and filter by city, state or type and get names, addresses, coordinates and contact details as JSON.\n\n\\#beer \\#brewery \\#craft-beer\n\n[Visit Official Docs](https://www.openbrewerydb.org/documentation?utm_source=freeapihub.com&utm_medium=referral)\n\nQuick facts\n\nAccess Free\n\nAuth No Auth\n\nFormat REST\n\nRegion United States only\n\nNo ratings yet — be the first\n\nData\n\nBreweries\n\n\\+ coordinates\n\nSearch\n\nCity/state/type\n\n\\+ query\n\nPricing\n\nFree\n\nfair use\n\nOverview Code Examples Live Tester Endpoints Reviews Alternatives\n\n## What is Open Brewery DB?\nOpen Brewery DB is a free, open API and dataset of **breweries, cideries, brewpubs and taprooms** around the world. You can **search by name, filter by city, state, country or type** , fetch a single brewery, or pull a random one, and each result includes the **name, brewery type, full address, coordinates, phone and website** . It needs **no API key** and returns clean JSON, making it ideal for beer-finder apps, maps, travel guides and directories. Because every record is geocoded, you can sort by distance or drop pins straight onto a map without a separate geocoding step.\n\n## Authentication & setup\n\nThere is **no authentication** . The base URL is `https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1/breweries` . List with pagination using `page` and `per_page` , search with `?by_city=` , `?by_state=` , `?by_type=` or the free-text `?query=` , fetch one by id at `/breweries/{id}` , and get a random brewery from `/breweries/random` . Everything returns JSON arrays or objects.\n\n## What it returns\nEach brewery includes its **name** , **brewery\\_type** (micro, brewpub, regional, large and so on), street **address** with city, state and postal code, **longitude and latitude** , **phone** and **website\\_url** . The coordinates make it easy to plot breweries on a map or compute the nearest ones, while the type lets you filter, say, brewpubs you can eat at from production-only sites. That single type field quietly answers the most common user question of whether they can actually visit and drink there.\n\n## Pricing & limits\n\nThe API is **free** with no key. There is a light, fair-use **rate limit** , and since the brewery dataset changes slowly, it **caches very well** — pull the breweries for a city or region once and store them, so a map or directory runs from a local copy. As an open project, caching is both faster and considerate of the service.\n\n## Best practices\n\n...\n\n* Place names must match the data\n\nQuick info\n\nBase URL https://api.openbrewerydb.org/v1\n\nAuth No Auth\n\nFormat REST\n\nVersion 1\\.0\n\nRegion United States only\n\n♡ Save to Collection\n\nShare\n\n[](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Open%20Brewery%20DB%20API%20%E2%80%94%20via%20%40FreeAPIHub) [](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=) Copy link\n\nGetting started\n\n## How to use Open Brewery DB API\n\n1\n\n### 🔍 Pick a filter\n\nChoose a filter — by\\_city, by\\_state, or by\\_type — to narrow the brewery search.\n\n2\n\n### 📡 Call the API\n\nCall GET /v1/breweries with your filters to fetch matching breweries as JSON, no key needed.\n\n3\n\n### 📄 Page results\n\nPage through large result sets with the page and per\\_page parameters.\n\n4\n\n### 📍 Map coordinates\n\nRead each brewery's latitude and longitude to plot it on a map.\n\n5\n\n### 💾 Cache data\n\nCache results per region, since the brewery data is fairly static and changes slowly.\n\nInspiration"

This API is perfect for developers who are building beer-related applications.\n\n## API Endpoints\nOpenBreweryDB provides several endpoints that allow you to retrieve information about breweries in various ways. These endpoints include:\n\n* `/breweries` : retrieves all the breweries in the database.\n* `/breweries?by_city=city_name` : retrieves all the breweries in the specified city.\n* `/breweries?by_name=brewery_name` : retrieves all breweries with the specified name.\n* `/breweries/{id}` : retrieves a brewery with a specific ID.\n\n## API Example in JavaScript\n\nTo use the OpenBreweryDB API in your JavaScript application, you need to send an HTTP request to the API. You can do this by using the `fetch` method.\n\nHere's an example of how to retrieve all the breweries in the database:\n\n```\nconst  url =  'https://api.openbrewerydb.org/breweries' ;\n\n fetch (url)\n  . then ( response  =>  response. json ())\n  . then ( data  =>  {\n     // Do something with the data \n  });\n```\n\nHere's an example of how to retrieve breweries in a specific city:\n\n```\nconst  city =  'Los Angeles' ;\nconst  url =  `https://api.openbrewerydb.org/breweries?by_city= ${city} ` ;\n\n fetch (url)\n  . then ( response  =>  response. json ())\n  . then ( data  =>  {\n     // Do something with the data \n  });\n```\n\nHere's an example of how to retrieve a specific brewery by ID:\n\n```\nconst  breweryId =  777 ;\n const  url =  `https://api.openbrewerydb.org/breweries/ ${breweryId} ` ;\n\n fetch (url)\n  . then ( response  =>  response. json ())\n  . then ( data  =>  {\n     // Do something with the data \n  });\n```\n\n## Conclusion\n\nOpenBreweryDB API is a great resource for developers who are building beer-related applications.         "Sort the results by distance from an origin point, denoted by `latitude,longitude` .\n\nRun request\n\n### `by_ids` \\#\n\nComma-separated list of brewery IDs.\n\nRun request\n\n### `by_name` \\#\n\nFilter breweries by name.\n\n**Note:** For the parameters, you can use underscores or [url encoding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Percent-encoding) for spaces.\n\nRun request\n\n### `by_state` \\#\n\nFilter breweries by state.\n\n**Note:** Full state name is required; no abbreviations. For the parameters, you can use underscores or [url encoding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Percent-encoding) for spaces.\n\nRun request\n\n### `by_postal` \\#\n\nFilter breweries by postal code.\n\nMay be filtered by basic (5 digit) postal code or more precisely filtered by postal+4 (9 digit) code.\n\n**Note:** If filtering by postal+4 the search must include either a hyphen or an underscore.\n\nRun request\n\n### `by_type` \\#\n\nFilter by type of brewery.\n\nMust be one of:\n\n* `micro` \\- Most craft breweries.\nFor example, Samual Adams is still considered a micro brewery.\n* `nano` \\- An extremely small brewery which typically only distributes locally.\n* `regional` \\- A regional location of an expanded brewery. Ex. Sierra Nevada’s Asheville, NC location.\n* `brewpub` \\- A beer-focused restaurant or restaurant/bar with a brewery on-premise.\n* `large` \\- A very large brewery. Likely not for visitors. Ex. Miller-Coors. (deprecatedated)\n* `planning` \\- A brewery in planning or not yet opened to the public.\n* `bar` \\- A bar. No brewery equipment on premise. (deprecatedated)\n* `contract` \\- A brewery that uses another brewery’s equipment.\n* `proprietor` \\- Similar to contract brewing but refers more to a brewery incubator.\n* `closed` \\- A location which has been closed.\n\nRun request\n\n### `page` \\#\n\nThe offset or page of breweries to return.\n\nRun request\n\n### `per_page` \\#\n\nNumber of breweries to return each call.\n\n**Note:** Default per page 50. Max per page is 200.\n\nRun request\n\n### `sort` \\#\nSort the results by one or more fields.\n\n* `asc` for ascending order\n* `desc` for descending order\n\n**Note:** `by_dist` does not work with the `sort` filter since it is a filter of its own.\n\nRun request\n\n#### Response Fields \\#\n\n|Field |Type |Description |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n|`id` |string |Unique identifier for the brewery |\n|`name` |string |Brewery name |\n|`brewery_type` |string |Type of brewery (see by\\_type for possible values) |\n|`address_1` |string or null |Primary street address |\n|`address_2` |string or null |Secondary address line |\n|`address_3` |string or null |Third address line |\n|`city` |string |City name |\n|`state_province` |string |State or province |\n|`postal_code` |string |Postal or ZIP code |\n|`country` |string |Country name |\n|`longitude` |number or null |Longitude coordinate |\n|`latitude` |number or null |Latitude coordinate |\n|`phone` |string or null |Contact phone number |\n|`website_url` |string or null |Brewery website URL |"

<skill_content name="deploy-to-vercel">
# Skill: deploy-to-vercel

# Deploy to Vercel

Deploy any project to Vercel. **Always deploy as preview** (not production) unless the user explicitly asks for production.

The goal is to get the user into the best long-term setup: their project linked to Vercel with git-push deploys. Every method below tries to move the user closer to that state.

## Step 1: Gather Project State

Run all four checks before deciding which method to use:

```bash
# 1. Check for a git remote
git remote get-url origin 2>/dev/null

# 2. Check if locally linked to a Vercel project (either file means linked)
cat .vercel/project.json 2>/dev/null || cat .vercel/repo.json 2>/dev/null

# 3. Check if the Vercel CLI is installed and authenticated
vercel whoami 2>/dev/null

# 4. List available teams (if authenticated)
vercel teams list --format json 2>/dev/null
```

### Team selection

If the user belongs to multiple teams, present all available team slugs as a bulleted list and ask which one to deploy to. Once the user picks a team, proceed immediately to the next step — do not ask for additional confirmation.

Pass the team slug via `--scope` on all subsequent CLI commands (`vercel deploy`, `vercel link`, `vercel inspect`, etc.):

```bash
vercel deploy [path] -y --no-wait --scope <team-slug>
```

If the project is already linked (`.vercel/project.json` or `.vercel/repo.json` exists), the `orgId` in those files determines the team — no need to ask again. If there is only one team (or just a personal account), skip the prompt and use it directly.

**About the `.vercel/` directory:** A linked project has either:
- `.vercel/project.json` — created by `vercel link` (single project linking). Contains `projectId` and `orgId`.
- `.vercel/repo.json` — created by `vercel link --repo` (repo-based linking). Contains `orgId`, `remoteName`, and a `projects` array mapping directories to Vercel project IDs.

Either file means the project is linked. Check for both.

**Do NOT** use `vercel project inspect`, `vercel ls`, or `vercel link` to detect state in an unlinked directory — without a `.vercel/` config, they will interactively prompt (or with `--yes`, silently link as a side-effect). Only `vercel whoami` is safe to run anywhere.

## Step 2: Choose a Deploy Method

### Linked (`.vercel/` exists) + has git remote → Git Push

This is the ideal state. The project is linked and has git integration.

1. **Ask the user before pushing.** Never push without explicit approval:
   ```
   This project is connected to Vercel via git. I can commit and push to
   trigger a deployment. Want me to proceed?
   ```

2. **Commit and push:**
   ```bash
   git add .
   git commit -m "deploy: <description of changes>"
   git push
   ```
   Vercel automatically builds from the push. Non-production branches get preview deployments; the production branch (usually `main`) gets a production deployment.

3. **Retrieve the preview URL.** If the CLI is authenticated:
   ```bash
   sleep 5
   vercel ls --format json
   ```
   The JSON output has a `deployments` array. Find the latest entry — its `url` field is the preview URL.

   If the CLI is not authenticated, tell the user to check the Vercel dashboard or the commit status checks on their git provider for the preview URL.

---

### Linked (`.vercel/` exists) + no git remote → `vercel deploy`

The project is linked but there's no git repo. Deploy directly with the CLI.

```bash
vercel deploy [path] -y --no-wait
```

Use `--no-wait` so the CLI returns immediately with the deployment URL instead of blocking until the build finishes (builds can take a while). Then check on the deployment status with:

```bash
vercel inspect <deployment-url>
```

For production deploys (only if user explicitly asks):
```bash
vercel deploy [path] --prod -y --no-wait
```

---

### Not linked + CLI is authenticated → Link first, then deploy

The CLI is working but the project isn't linked yet. This is the opportunity to get the user into the best state.

1. **Ask the user which team to deploy to.** Present the team slugs from Step 1 as a bulleted list. If there's only one team (or just a personal account), skip this step.

2. **Once a team is selected, proceed directly to linking.** Tell the user what will happen but do not ask for separate confirmation:
   ```
   Linking this project to <team name> on Vercel. This will create a Vercel
   project to deploy to and enable automatic deployments on future git pushes.
   ```

3. **If a git remote exists**, use repo-based linking with the selected team scope:
   ```bash
   vercel link --repo --scope <team-slug>
   ```
   This reads the git remote URL and matches it to existing Vercel projects that deploy from that repo. It creates `.vercel/repo.json`. This is much more reliable than `vercel link` (without `--repo`), which tries to match by directory name and often fails when the local folder and Vercel project are named differently.

   **If there is no git remote**, fall back to standard linking:
   ```bash
   vercel link --scope <team-slug>
   ```
   This prompts the user to select or create a project. It creates `.vercel/project.json`.

4. **Then deploy using the best available method:**
   - If a git remote exists → commit and push (see git push method above)
   - If no git remote → `vercel deploy [path] -y --no-wait --scope <team-slug>`, then `vercel inspect <url>` to check status

---

### Not linked + CLI not authenticated → Install, auth, link, deploy

The Vercel CLI isn't set up at all.

1. **Install the CLI (if not already installed):**
   ```bash
   npm install -g vercel
   ```

2. **Authenticate:**
   ```bash
   vercel login
   ```
   The user completes authentication in their browser. If interactive authentication is unavailable or fails, stop and explain that an authenticated Vercel CLI session is required. Do not package or upload the project through another service.

3. **Ask which team to deploy to** — present team slugs from `vercel teams list --format json` as a bulleted list. If only one team / personal account, skip. Once selected, proceed immediately.

4. **Link the project** with the selected team scope (use `--repo` if a git remote exists, plain `vercel link` otherwise):
   ```bash
   vercel link --repo --scope <team-slug>   # if git remote exists
   vercel link --scope <team-slug>          # if no git remote
   ```

5. **Deploy** using the best available method (git push if remote exists, otherwise `vercel deploy -y --no-wait --scope <team-slug>`, then `vercel inspect <url>` to check status).

---

### Authentication unavailable → Stop

Do not deploy without an authenticated Vercel CLI session. Do not run bundled fallback scripts, send the project to an unauthenticated deployment endpoint, or upload an archive through another service.

Tell the user that deployment stopped because Vercel authentication is required. They can run `vercel login` and retry after authentication succeeds.

---

## Output

Always show the user the deployment URL.

- **Git push:** Use `vercel ls --format json` to find the preview URL. If the CLI isn't authenticated, tell the user to check the Vercel dashboard or commit status checks.
- **CLI deploy:** Show the URL returned by `vercel deploy --no-wait`. Use `vercel inspect <url>` to check build status and report it to the user.

**Do not** curl or fetch the deployed URL to verify it works. Just return the link.

---

## Troubleshooting

### Network Access Error

If deployment fails because network access is unavailable, report the failure and stop. Do not route the project through a different deployment endpoint.

### CLI Auth Failure

If `vercel login` or `vercel deploy` fails with authentication errors, report that an authenticated Vercel CLI session is required and stop.

Base directory for this skill: file:///home/anton/.agents/skills/deploy-to-vercel
Relative paths in this skill (e.g., scripts/, reference/) are relative to this base directory.
Note: file list is sampled.

<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/deploy-to-vercel/local.patch</file>
</skill_files>
</skill_content>
```

16: 
17: # Create Pull Request
18: 
19: This skill guides you through creating a well-structured GitHub pull request that follows project conventions and best practices.
20: 
21: ## Prerequisites Check
22: 
23: Before proceeding, verify the following:
24: 
25: ### 1. Check if `gh` CLI is installed
26: 
27: ```bash
28: gh --version
29: ```
30: 
31: If not installed, inform the user:
32: > The GitHub CLI (`gh`) is required but not installed. Please install it:
33: > - macOS: `brew install gh`
34: > - Other: https://cli.github.com/
35: 
36: ### 2. Check if authenticated with GitHub
37: 
38: ```bash
39: gh auth status
40: ```
41: 
42: If not authenticated, guide the user to run `gh auth login`.
43: 
44: ### 3. Verify clean working directory
45: 
46: ```bash
47: git status
48: ```
49: 
50: If there are uncommitted changes, ask the user whether to:
51: - Commit them as part of this PR
52: - Stash them temporarily
53: - Discard them (with caution)
54: 
55: ## Gather Context
56: 
57: ### 1. Identify the current branch
58: 
59: ```bash
60: git branch --show-current
61: ```
62: 
63: Ensure you're not on `main` or `master`. If so, ask the user to create or switch to a feature branch.
64: 
65: ### 2. Find the base branch
66: 
67: ```bash
68: git remote show origin | grep "HEAD branch"
69: ```
70: 
71: This is typically `main` or `master`.
72: 
73: ### 3. Analyze recent commits relevant to this PR
74: 
75: ```bash
76: git log origin/main..HEAD --oneline --no-decorate
77: ```
78: 
79: Review these commits to understand:
80: - What changes are being introduced
81: - The scope of the PR (single feature/fix or multiple changes)
82: - Whether commits should be squashed or reorganized
83: 
84: ### 4. Review the diff
85: 
86: ```bash
87: git diff origin/main..HEAD --stat
88: ```
89: 
90: This shows which files changed and helps identify the type of change.
91: 
92: ## Information Gathering
93: 
94: Before creating the PR, you need the following information. Check if it can be inferred from:
95: - Commit messages
96: - Branch name (e.g., `fix/issue-123`, `feature/new-login`)
97: - Changed files and their content
98: 
99: If any critical information is missing, use `ask_followup_question` to ask the user:
100: 
101: ### Required Information
102: 
103: 1. **Related Issue Number**: Look for patterns like `#123`, `fixes #123`, or `closes #123` in commit messages
104: 2. **Description**: What problem does this solve? Why were these changes made?
105: 3. **Type of Change**: Bug fix, new feature, breaking change, refactor, cosmetic, documentation, or workflow
106: 4. **Test Procedure**: How was this tested? What could break?
107: 
108: ### Example clarifying question
109: 
110: If the issue number is not found:
111: > I couldn't find a related issue number in the commit messages or branch name. What GitHub issue does this PR address? (Enter the issue number, e.g., "123" or "N/A" for small fixes)
112: 
113: ## Git Best Practices
114: 
115: Before creating the PR, consider these best practices:
116: 
117: ### Commit Hygiene
118: 
119: 1. **Atomic commits**: Each commit should represent a single logical change
120: 2. **Clear commit messages**: Follow conventional commit format when possible
121: 3. **No merge commits**: Prefer rebasing over merging to keep history clean
122: 
123: ### Branch Management
124: 
125: 1. **Rebase on latest main** (if needed):
126:    ```bash
127:    git fetch origin
128:    git rebase origin/main
129:    ```
130: 
131: 2. **Squash if appropriate**: If there are many small "WIP" commits, consider interactive rebase:
132:    ```bash
133:    git rebase -i origin/main
134:    ```
135:    Only suggest this if commits appear messy and the user is comfortable with rebasing.
136: 
137: ### Push Changes
138: 
139: Ensure all commits are pushed:
140: ```bash
141: git push origin HEAD
142: ```
143: 
144: If the branch was rebased, you may need:
145: ```bash
146: git push origin HEAD --force-with-lease
147: ```
148: 
149: ## Create the Pull Request
150: 
151: **IMPORTANT**: Read and use the PR template at `.github/pull_request_template.md`. The PR body format must **strictly match** the template structure. Do not deviate from the template format.
152: 
153: When filling out the template:
154: - Replace `#XXXX` with the actual issue number, or keep as `#XXXX` if no issue exists (for small fixes)
155: - Fill in all sections with relevant information gathered from commits and context
156: - Mark the appropriate "Type of Change" checkbox(es)
157: - Complete the "Pre-flight Checklist" items that apply
158: 
159: ### Create PR with gh CLI
160: 
161: **Use a temporary file for the PR body** to avoid shell escaping issues, newline problems, and other command-line flakiness:
162: 
163: 1. Write the PR body to a temporary file:
164:    ```
165:    /tmp/pr-body.md
166:    ```
167: 
168: 2. Create the PR using the file:
169:    ```bash
170:    gh pr create --title "PR_TITLE" --body-file /tmp/pr-body.md --base main
171:    ```
172: 
173: 3. Clean up the temporary file:
174:    ```bash
175:    rm /tmp/pr-body.md
176:    ```
177: 
178: For draft PRs:
179: ```bash
180: gh pr create --title "PR_TITLE" --body-file /tmp/pr-body.md --base main --draft
181: ```
182: 
183: **Why use a file?** Passing complex markdown with newlines, special characters, and checkboxes directly via `--body` is error-prone. The `--body-file` flag handles all content reliably.
184: 
185: ## Post-Creation
186: 
187: After creating the PR:
188: 
189: 1. **Display the PR URL** so the user can review it
190: 2. **Remind about CI checks**: Tests and linting will run automatically
191: 3. **Suggest next steps**:
192:    - Add reviewers if needed: `gh pr edit --add-reviewer USERNAME`
193:    - Add labels if needed: `gh pr edit --add-label "bug"`
194: 
195: ## Error Handling
196: 
197: ### Common Issues
198: 
199: 1. **No commits ahead of main**: The branch has no changes to submit
200:    - Ask if the user meant to work on a different branch
201: 
202: 2. **Branch not pushed**: Remote doesn't have the branch
203:    - Push the branch first: `git push -u origin HEAD`
204: 
205: 3. **PR already exists**: A PR for this branch already exists
206:    - Show the existing PR: `gh pr view`
207:    - Ask if they want to update it instead
208: 
209: 4. **Merge conflicts**: Branch conflicts with base
210:    - Guide user through resolving conflicts or rebasing
211: 
212: ## Summary Checklist
213: 
214: Before finalizing, ensure:
215: - [ ] `gh` CLI is installed and authenticated
216: - [ ] Working directory is clean
217: - [ ] All commits are pushed
218: - [ ] Branch is up-to-date with base branch
219: - [ ] Related issue number is identified, or placeholder is used
220: - [ ] PR description follows the template exactly
221: - [ ] Appropriate type of change is selected
222: - [ ] Pre-flight checklist items are addressed

(End of file - total 222 lines)
</content>
```

**Step 4 — Deploy to Vercel:**
```bash
vercel login
vercel link --repo
vercel deploy -y --no-wait
```

**Step 5 — Get the preview URL:**
```bash
vercel ls --format json | jq -r '.[0].url'
```

3: const express = require('express');
