284:               <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Location</h2>
285:               <Map
286:                 breweries={[brewery]}
287:                 center={[brewery.latitude, brewery.longitude]}
288:                 zoom={15}
289:                 selectedBrewery={brewery}
290:                 height="400px"
291:               />
292:             </div>
293:           )}
294: 
295:           <BeersSection breweryId={brewery.id} />
296:         </div>
297:       </div>
298: 
299:       <div className="grid grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-8">
300:         <div className="lg:col-span-2">
301:           <h2 className="text-2xl font-bold text-gray-900 mb-6">Reviews</h2>
302:           {reviewsLoading ? (
303:             <div className="space-y-4">
304:               {[1, 2].map((i) => (
305:                 <div key={i} className="bg-white rounded-xl p-5 animate-pulse">
306:                   <div className="h-4 bg-gray-200 rounded w-1/4 mb-2" />
307:                   <div className="h-3 bg-gray-200 rounded w-3/4" />
308:                 </div>
309:               ))}
310:             </div>
311:           ) : reviews.length === 0 ? (
312:             <div className="bg-white rounded-xl border border-gray-100 p-8 text-center">
313:               <div className="text-4xl mb-3">💬</div>
314:               <p className="text-gray-500">No reviews yet. Be the first to share your experience!</p>
315:             </div>
316:           ) : (
317:             <div className="space-y-4">
318:               {reviews.map((review) => (
319:                 <div key={review.id} className="bg-white rounded-xl shadow-sm border border-gray-100 p-5 hover:shadow-md transition-shadow">
320:                   <div className="flex items-center gap-3 mb-3">
321:                     <div className="w-10 h-10 bg-primary-500 text-white rounded-full flex items-center justify-center text-sm font-bold">
322:                       {(review.userName || review.user?.name || 'U')[0].toUpperCase()}
323:                     </div>
324:                     <div>
325:                       <p className="font-semibold text-gray-900 text-sm">{review.userName || review.user?.name || 'User'}</p>
326:                       <ReviewStars rating={review.rating} />
327:                     </div>
328:                   </div>
329:                   {review.comment && <p className="text-gray-700 text-sm mt-2 leading-relaxed">{review.comment}</p>}
330:                   <p className="text-xs text-gray-400 mt-3">{new Date(review.createdAt).toLocaleDateString()}</p>
331:                 </div>
332:               ))}
333:             </div>
334:           )}
335:         </div>
336: 
337:         <div>
338:           {user && (
339:             <div className="bg-white rounded-xl shadow-sm border border-gray-100 p-6 sticky top-24">
340:               <h3 className="text-lg font-bold text-gray-900 mb-4">Write a Review</h3>
341:               <form onSubmit={handleSubmitReview} className="space-y-4">
342:                 <div>
343:                   <label className="block text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 mb-1.5">Rating</label>
344:                   <select
345:                     value={reviewForm.rating}
346:                     onChange={(e) => setReviewForm({ ...reviewForm, rating: parseInt(e.target.value) })}
347:                     className="w-full px-3 py-2.5 rounded-lg border border-gray-200 focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-primary-500"
348:                   >
349:                     {[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].map((n) => (
350:                       <option key={n} value={n}>{n} Star{n > 1 ? 's' : ''}</option>
351:                     ))}
352:                   </select>
353:                 </div>
354:                 <div>
355:                   <label className="block text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 mb-1.5">Comment</label>
356:                   <textarea
357:                     value={reviewForm.comment}
358:                     onChange={(e) => setReviewForm({ ...reviewForm, comment: e.target.value })}
359:                     rows={4}
360:                     className="w-full px-4 py-2.5 rounded-lg border border-gray-200 focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-primary-500 resize-none"
361:                     placeholder="Share your experience..."
362:                   />
363:                 </div>
364:                 <button
365:                   type="submit"
366:                   disabled={submittingReview}
367:                   className="w-full bg-primary-500 hover:bg-primary-600 disabled:bg-primary-400 text-white font-medium px-4 py-2.5 rounded-lg transition-colors"
368:                 >
369:                   {submittingReview ? 'Submitting...' : 'Submit Review'}
370:                 </button>
371:               </form>
372:             </div>
373:           )}
374:         </div>
375:       </div>
376:     </div>
377:   );
378: }
379: 
380: function BeersSection({ breweryId }) {
381:   const [beers, setBeers] = useState([]);
382:   const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
383: 
384:   useEffect(() => {
385:     const fetchBeers = async () => {
386:       try {
387:         const res = await fetch(`/api/beers/brewery/${breweryId}`);
388:         if (res.ok) {
389:           const data = await res.json();
390:           setBeers(data.data || []);
391:         }
392:       } catch (e) {
393:         console.error('Failed to fetch beers:', e);
394:       } finally {
395:         setLoading(false);
396:       }
397:     };
398:     fetchBeers();
399:   }, [breweryId]);
400: 
401:   if (loading) {
402:     return (
403:       <div className="mb-8">
404:         <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers</h2>
405:         <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
406:           {[1, 2, 3].map((i) => (
407:             <div key={i} className="bg-gray-50 rounded-xl p-4 animate-pulse">
408:               <div className="h-5 bg-gray-200 rounded mb-2" />
409:               <div className="h-4 bg-gray-200 rounded w-2/3" />
410:             </div>
411:           ))}
412:         </div>
413:       </div>
414:     );
415:   }
416: 
417:   if (beers.length === 0) {
418:     return (
419:       <div className="mb-8">
420:         <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers</h2>
421:         <p className="text-gray-500">No beers listed for this brewery yet.</p>
422:       </div>
423:     );
424:   }
425: 
426:   return (
427:     <div className="mb-8">
428:       <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers ({beers.length})</h2>
429:       <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
430:         {beers.map((beer) => (
431:           <Link
432:             key={beer.id}
433:             to={`/beers/${beer.id}`}
434:             className="bg-gray-50 rounded-xl p-4 hover:bg-gray-100 transition-colors"
435:           >
436:             <div className="flex items-start justify-between mb-2">
437:               <h3 className="font-semibold text-gray-900">{beer.name}</h3>
438:               <span className="text-2xl">🍺</span>
439:             </div>
440:             {beer.style && (
441:               <span className="inline-block px-2 py-0.5 bg-amber-100 text-amber-800 rounded-full text-xs font-medium mb-2">
442:                 {beer.style}
443:               </span>
444:             )}
445:             <div className="flex items-center gap-3 text-sm text-gray-600">
446:               {beer.abv && <span>ABV: {beer.abv}%</span>}
447:               {beer.ibu && <span>IBU: {beer.ibu}</span>}
448:             </div>
449:           </Link>
450:         ))}
451:       </div>
452:     </div>
453:   );
454: }

## Assistant (Orchestrator · Auto Free · 17.1s)

_Thinking:_

All tasks are complete. Let me provide a comprehensive summary of everything that was built.

Everything is in place. Here's a summary of what was added:

### Backend

**Beer model** (`server/database/schema.sql`, `server/database/seed.sql`, `server/routes/beers.js`, `server/server.js`):
- `beers` table with fields: `brewery_id`, `name`, `style`, `abv`, `ibu`, `description`, `image_url`
- 12 seeded beers across US, Ukrainian, and Czech breweries
- API routes: `GET /api/beers`, `GET /api/beers/:id`, `GET /api/beers/brewery/:breweryId`, `GET /api/beers/meta/styles`

**Challenge system** (`server/database/schema.sql`, `server/database/seed.sql`, `server/routes/challenges.js`, `server/server.js`):
- `challenges` table with `title`, `description`, `difficulty`, `points`, `category`
- `user_challenges` table tracking user progress (`pending`/`completed`)
- 12 seeded challenges (beginner, style, social, travel categories)
- API routes: `GET /api/challenges`, `GET /api/challenges/random`, `GET /api/challenges/:id`, `GET /api/challenges/my-challenges`, `POST /api/challenges/:id/accept`, `POST /api/challenges/:id/complete`

### Frontend

**Beer pages:**
- **`BeerDetail.jsx`** — dedicated page showing beer name, style, ABV, IBU, description, and brewery link
- **`BreweryDetail.jsx`** — now includes a `BeersSection` that lists all beers for the brewery with links to their detail pages

**Challenge gamification:**
- **`ChallengeRoulette.jsx`** — animated roulette wheel that spins and lands on a random challenge
- **`ChallengeCard.jsx`** — card displaying challenge title, description, difficulty, points, and accept button
- **`ChallengeCompletionModal.jsx`** — modal for completing challenges with photo upload and optional review/rating
- **`Challenges.jsx`** — full challenges page with roulette, "My Challenges" section, and filterable challenge grid
- **`Navbar.jsx`** — added Challenges navigation link

**Routes updated** (`App.jsx`):
- `/beers/:id` → BeerDetail
- `/challenges` → Challenges

### How to run

```bash
cd /home/anton/projects/beer-road
npm run install:all
npm run dev
```

Then visit:
- `http://localhost:5173/challenges` — spin the roulette and accept challenges
- `http://localhost:5173/breweries/:id` — scroll down to see the Beers section
- `http://localhost:5173/beers/:id` — view individual beer details

```bash
cd /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client

# Install Playwright (if not already installed)
npm install -D @playwright/test

# Install Chromium browser
npx playwright install chromium

# Run tests
npx playwright test

# View HTML report
npx playwright show-report
```

The tests will automatically start the Vite dev server on port 5173, run all 9 tests in Chromium, and generate an HTML report. All tests are currently written as read-only checks (no login or data mutation required) so they should pass without any setup.

## Assistant (Orchestrator · Auto Free · 11.6s)

_Thinking:_

**Tool: skill**

**Input:**
```json
{
  "name": "webapp-testing"
}
```

**Output:**
```
<skill_content name="webapp-testing">
# Skill: webapp-testing

# Web Application Testing

To test local web applications, write native Python Playwright scripts.

**Helper Scripts Available**:
- `scripts/with_server.py` - Manages server lifecycle (supports multiple servers)

**Always run scripts with `--help` first** to see usage. DO NOT read the source until you try running the script first and find that a customized solution is abslutely necessary. These scripts can be very large and thus pollute your context window. They exist to be called directly as black-box scripts rather than ingested into your context window.

## Decision Tree: Choosing Your Approach

```
User task → Is it static HTML?
    ├─ Yes → Read HTML file directly to identify selectors
    │         ├─ Success → Write Playwright script using selectors
    │         └─ Fails/Incomplete → Treat as dynamic (below)
    │
    └─ No (dynamic webapp) → Is the server already running?
        ├─ No → Run: python scripts/with_server.py --help
        │        Then use the helper + write simplified Playwright script
        │
        └─ Yes → Reconnaissance-then-action:
            1. Navigate and wait for networkidle
            2. Take screenshot or inspect DOM
            3. Identify selectors from rendered state
            4. Execute actions with discovered selectors
```

## Example: Using with_server.py

To start a server, run `--help` first, then use the helper:

**Single server:**
```bash
python scripts/with_server.py --server "npm run dev" --port 5173 -- python your_automation.py
```

**Multiple servers (e.g., backend + frontend):**
```bash
python scripts/with_server.py \
  --server "cd backend && python server.py" --port 3000 \
  --server "cd frontend && npm run dev" --port 5173 \
  -- python your_automation.py
```

To create an automation script, include only Playwright logic (servers are managed automatically):
```python
from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

with sync_playwright() as p:
    browser = p.chromium.launch(headless=True) # Always launch chromium in headless mode
    page = browser.new_page()
    page.goto('http://localhost:5173') # Server already running and ready
    page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle') # CRITICAL: Wait for JS to execute
    # ... your automation logic
    browser.close()
```

## Reconnaissance-Then-Action Pattern

1. **Inspect rendered DOM**:
   ```python
   page.screenshot(path='/tmp/inspect.png', full_page=True)
   content = page.content()
   page.locator('button').all()
   ```

2. **Identify selectors** from inspection results

3. **Execute actions** using discovered selectors

## Common Pitfall

❌ **Don't** inspect the DOM before waiting for `networkidle` on dynamic apps
✅ **Do** wait for `page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')` before inspection

## Best Practices

- **Use bundled scripts as black boxes** - To accomplish a task, consider whether one of the scripts available in `scripts/` can help. These scripts handle common, complex workflows reliably without cluttering the context window. Use `--help` to see usage, then invoke directly.
- Use `sync_playwright()` for synchronous scripts
- Always close the browser when done
- Use descriptive selectors: `text=`, `role=`, CSS selectors, or IDs
- Add appropriate waits: `page.wait_for_selector()` or `page.wait_for_timeout()`

## Reference Files

- **examples/** - Examples showing common patterns:
  - `element_discovery.py` - Discovering buttons, links, and inputs on a page
  - `static_html_automation.py` - Using file:// URLs for local HTML
  - `console_logging.py` - Capturing console logs during automation

It enables large language models (LLMs) to interact with web pages, take screenshots, and execute JavaScript in a real browser environment.\n\n## How to use MCP Server Playwright?\n\nInstall the server via Smithery ( `npx -y @smithery/cli install @automatalabs/mcp-server-playwright --client claude` ) or using npx ( `npx @automatalabs/mcp-server-playwright install` ) or mcp-get. The installation process checks OS compatibility and automatically creates or updates the Claude desktop configuration file at the appropriate path. The required configuration adds a `playwright` entry under `mcpServers` in the Claude config.\n\n## Key features of MCP Server Playwright\n\n* Full browser automation capabilities\n* Screenshot capture of entire pages or specific elements\n* Comprehensive web interaction (navigation, clicking, form filling)\n* Console log monitoring\n* JavaScript execution in browser context\n\n## Use cases of MCP Server Playwright\n\n* LLM-driven web page navigation and data extraction\n* Visual feedback through screenshot capture for LLM agents\n* Automated form filling and element interaction\n* Executing custom JavaScript in the browser console\n* Monitoring browser console logs for debugging\n\n## FAQ from MCP Server Playwright\n\n### What tools does MCP Server Playwright provide?\n\nIt provides tools for navigation ( `browser_navigate` ), screenshot capture ( `browser_screenshot` ), clicking elements ( `browser_click` , `browser_click_text` ), hovering ( `browser_hover` , `browser_hover_text` ), filling input fields ( `browser_fill` ), selecting options ( `browser_select` , `browser_select_text` ), and executing JavaScript ( `browser_evaluate` ).\n\n### How do I capture a screenshot?"

        "# microsoft/playwright-mcp\n\n- Page: GitHub repository\n- URL: https://github.com/microsoft/playwright-mcp\n- Description: Playwright MCP server\n- Stars: 34,991\n- Forks: 2,906\n- Topics: mcp, playwright\n- License: Apache-2.0 license\n- Default branch: main\n- Created: 2025-03-21T17:48:36.000Z\n- Commits: 564\n\n## Top-level files\n\n- .claude/skills/\n- .devcontainer/\n- .github/workflows/\n- src/\n- tests/\n- .gitignore\n- .npmignore\n- CLAUDE.md\n- CONTRIBUTING.md\n- Dockerfile\n- LICENSE\n- README.md\n- SECURITY.md\n- cli.js\n- config.d.ts\n- index.d.ts\n- index.js\n- package-lock.json\n- package.json\n- playwright.config.ts\n- roll.js\n- server.json\n- update-readme.js\n\n## README.md\n\n## Playwright MCP\n\nA Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that provides browser automation capabilities using [Playwright](https://playwright.dev/) . This server enables LLMs to interact with web pages through structured accessibility snapshots, bypassing the need for screenshots or visually-tuned models.\n### Playwright MCP vs Playwright CLI\n\nThis package provides MCP interface into Playwright. If you are using a **coding agent** , you might benefit from using the [CLI+SKILLS](https://github.com/microsoft/playwright-cli) instead.\n\n* **CLI** : Modern **coding agents** increasingly favor CLI–based workflows exposed as SKILLs over MCP because CLI invocations are more token-efficient: they avoid loading large tool schemas and verbose accessibility trees into the model context, allowing agents to act through concise, purpose-built commands. This makes CLI + SKILLs better suited for high-throughput coding agents that must balance browser automation with large codebases, tests, and reasoning within limited context windows.  \n  **Learn more about [Playwright CLI with SKILLS](https://github.com/microsoft/playwright-cli)** .\n* **MCP** : MCP remains relevant for specialized agentic loops that benefit from persistent state, rich introspection, and iterative reasoning over page structure, such as exploratory automation, self-healing tests, or long-running autonomous workflows where maintaining continuous browser context outweighs token cost concerns.\n\n### Key Features\n\n* **Fast and lightweight** . Uses Playwright's accessibility tree, not pixel-based input.\n* **LLM-friendly** . No vision models needed, operates purely on structured data.\n* **Deterministic tool application** . Avoids ambiguity common with screenshot-based approaches.\n\n### Requirements\n\n* Node.js 18 or newer\n* VS Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Desktop, Goose, Grok, Junie or any other MCP client\n\n### Getting started\n\nFirst, install the Playwright MCP server with your client.\n\n**Standard config** works in most of the tools:\n\n```js\n{\n  \"mcpServers\": {\n    \"playwright\": {\n      \"command\": \"npx\",\n      \"args\": [\n        \"@playwright/mcp@latest\""
      ]

This operation will automatically create a new session if there is no active session.\n### `playwright_screenshot`\n\nTakes a screenshot of the current page or a specific element.\n\n### `playwright_click`\n\nClicks an element on the page using a CSS selector.\n\n### `playwright_fill`\n\nFills out an input field.\n\n### `playwright_evaluate`\n\nExecutes JavaScript code in the browser console.\n\n### `playwright_click_text`\n\nClicks an element on the page by its text content.\n\n### `playwright_get_text_content`\n\nGet the text content of all visiable elements.\n\n### `playwright_get_html_content`\n\nGet the HTML content of the page.\n\n## Overview\n\n## What is playwright-server?\n\nA Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that provides Playwright-based browser automation tools for navigation, screenshots, clicking, form filling, JavaScript execution, and content extraction. It also includes a simple note storage system with a summarization prompt.\n\n## How to use playwright-server?\n\nConfigure the server in your MCP client (e.g.\n, Claude Desktop) by adding the `playwright-server` entry to the `mcpServers` configuration. Use the published package with `uvx playwright-server` or for development use `uv --directory <path> run playwright-server` . The server exposes tools that can be invoked via the MCP tools interface.\n\n## Key features of playwright-server\n\n* Navigate to URLs with automatic session creation.\n* Take full-page or element-specific screenshots.\n* Click elements by CSS selector or text content.\n* Fill input fields with specified values.\n* Execute JavaScript in the browser console.\n* Retrieve visible text or HTML content of a page.\n* Store and access notes via custom note:// URI scheme.\n* Generate note summaries with adjustable detail level.\n\n## Use cases of playwright-server\n\n—\n\n## FAQ from playwright\n\n## Comments\n\n## More Browser Automation MCP servers\n\n### Fetch MCP jae-jae MCP server for fetch web page content using Playwright headless browser. 1\\."

      "excerpts": [
        "# ExecuteAutomation Playwright MCP Server\n\n> A community-built MCP server for browser and API automation using Playwright, with built-in API testing not found in the official Microsoft version.\n\nExecuteAutomation Playwright MCP Server is a community-built Model Context Protocol server that enables LLMs to automate browsers and test APIs using the Playwright framework. Created by ExecuteAutomation (Karthik KK) and first released in December 2024, it predates the official [Microsoft Playwright MCP](/tools/playwright-mcp) (@playwright/mcp) that launched in March 2025. This is not the official Playwright MCP from Microsoft — it is an independent, community-maintained alternative published under the `@executeautomation` npm scope.\n\nThe server provides MCP tools that let AI agents navigate web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, execute JavaScript, run API requests, and generate reusable Playwright test scripts through natural language prompts.\nIts main differentiator from the official Microsoft version is built-in API testing support (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) and CSS selector-based browser interaction, whereas the official version uses Playwright's accessibility tree for more deterministic element targeting. Teams that need both UI and API automation in a single MCP server may find this a practical choice, while those prioritizing reliability, deeper configuration options, and broader MCP client support may prefer the official Microsoft version (@playwright/mcp).\n\n- **Browser Navigation and Interaction** - Navigate to URLs with configurable viewport, browser type, and timeout settings, then click, hover, fill forms, select dropdowns, drag elements, and press keys using CSS selectors\n- **Multi-Browser Support** - Automate across Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit browser engines with seamless switching during sessions\n- **API Testing Tools** - Perform GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE requests with bearer token authentication and custom headers directly through MCP tools, a capability not available in the official Microsoft Playwright MCP\n- **Screenshot Capture** - Take full-page or element-specific screenshots with configurable dimensions and optional base64 output or PNG file saving\n- **Code Generation Sessions** - Record browser interactions and generate reusable Playwright test scripts with customizable output paths and test name prefixes\n- **Device Emulation** - Resize viewport to emulate 143 pre-configured device presets including iPhones, iPads, Pixels, Galaxy devices, and desktop browsers with proper user-agent and touch support\n- **Console Log Monitoring** - Retrieve and filter browser console logs by type including error, warning, info, debug, and exception levels\n- **JavaScript Execution** - Run arbitrary JavaScript in the browser console and retrieve results"
      ]
    },

The maintainer can list them under a `## Tools` heading to populate this section.\n\n## Overview\n\n## What is Playwright MCP Server 🎭?\n\nPlaywright MCP Server 🎭 is a Model Context Protocol server that provides browser automation capabilities using Playwright. It enables large language models to interact with web pages, take screenshots, generate test code, web scrape pages, and execute JavaScript in a real browser environment.\n\n## How to use Playwright MCP Server 🎭?\n\nInstall the package globally via npm, or use mcp-get, Smithery, or VS Code CLI. The server automatically installs browser binaries on first use. Configure it in stdio mode for Claude Desktop or HTTP mode for VS Code GitHub Copilot and custom clients. Invoke tools like `playwright_resize` with device presets.\n\n## Key features of Playwright MCP Server 🎭\n* Browser automation using Playwright (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit)\n* 143 real device presets with natural language support\n* Automatic browser installation on first use\n* HTTP mode for remote, multi-client, and VS Code integration\n* Screenshot capture, test code generation, web scraping, JavaScript execution\n* Health check endpoint and monitoring system\n\n## Use cases of Playwright MCP Server 🎭\n\n* Let LLMs browse the web and take screenshots for visual understanding\n* Generate Playwright test code from natural language instructions\n* Automate web scraping and data extraction tasks\n* Test responsive web designs across 143 device profiles\n* Enable headless browser access from remote servers or CI/CD pipelines\n\n## FAQ from Playwright MCP Server 🎭\n\n### How do I install Playwright MCP Server 🎭?\n\nYou can install it globally via npm ( `npm install -g @executeautomation/playwright-mcp-server` ), via Smithery, or through mcp-get."
      ]
    },
    {
      "url": "https://github.com/deepakkamboj/playwright-accessibility-mcp-server",
      "title": "GitHub - deepakkamboj/playwright-accessibility-mcp-server: Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for accessibility testing using Playwright and Axe. · GitHub",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "\"playwright-accessibility-mcp-server\" : {\n      \"command\" : \" node \" ,\n      \"args\" : [ \" ${AbsolutePath}/build/index.js \" ]\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\nOnce the MCP server is running, you can invoke tools like:\n\n* `scan-url` (params: `{ \"url\": \"https://google.com\" }` )\n* `scan-html` (params: `{ \"html\": \"<h1>Hello</h1>\" }` )\n* `scan-batch` (params: `{ \"urls\": [\"https://a.com\",\"https://b.com\"] }` )\n* `summarize-violations` (params: `{ \"result\": <axe result> }` )\n\n### MCP Local Dev Mode\n\nFor local development, configure your `<client>_config.json` as follows:\n\n```\n{\n  \"mcpServers\" : {\n    \"playwright-accessibility-mcp-server\" : {\n      \"command\" : \" node \" ,\n      \"args\" : [ \" ${AbsolutePath}/build/index.js \" ]\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\n## Development\n\n* To run the server in development mode:\n  \n  ```\n  npm run dev\n  ```\n* To test the tools, export the server and use the MCP SDK.\n\n## 🤝 Contributing\n\n1. Fork the repo.\n2. Create a branch ( `git checkout -b feature/xyz` ).\n3. Commit your changes.\n4. Open a Pull Request (PR).\n## License\n\nThis project is licensed under the MIT License.\n\n## About\n\nModel Context Protocol (MCP) server for accessibility testing using Playwright and Axe.\n\n### Resources\n\nReadme\n\n### License\n\nMIT license\n\nActivity\n\n### Stars\n\n**1** star\n\n### Watchers\n\n**1** watching\n\n### Forks\n\n**0** forks\n\n[Report repository](https://github.com/contact/report-content?content_url=https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fdeepakkamboj%2Fplaywright-accessibility-mcp-server&report=deepakkamboj+%28user%29)\n\n## Releases 2\n\nv0.1.4 Latest May 16, 2025\n\n\\+ 1 release\n\n## [Packages](https://github.com/users/deepakkamboj/packages?repo_name=playwright-accessibility-mcp-server)\n\n## Contributors\n\n* \n* \n*\n\n## Languages\n\n* [TypeScript 100\\.0%](https://github.com/deepakkamboj/playwright-accessibility-mcp-server/search?l=typescript)\n\n## Footer"

17: You are Kilo Code, an experienced technical leader who is inquisitive, skeptical, and an excellent planner.
18: 
19: Your job is to gather context, challenge assumptions, resolve design questions, and produce an implementation-ready plan that another agent can execute. You do not implement source-code changes.
20: 
21: 
22: Planning behavior:
23: 
24: - Inspect the codebase and available local context before asking questions that can be answered without the user.
25: - Interview the user relentlessly about every important aspect of the plan until you reach shared understanding.
26: - Walk down each branch of the design tree, resolving dependencies between decisions one by one.
27: - Ask one question at a time, and include your recommended answer.
28: - Do not optimize for a fixed number of questions. Continue until the important decisions are resolved or explicitly marked out of scope.
29: - Challenge vague or overloaded terms such as "user", "account", "tenant", "job", "workflow", "session", or "state" until their meaning is precise in this codebase.
30: - Cross-check user claims against the actual code and available context. If they conflict, call out the contradiction directly.
31: - Use concrete scenarios and edge cases to test the proposed design.
32: - Prefer short, actionable plans over long speculative documents.
33: - Never provide level-of-effort estimates such as hours, days, or weeks.
34: 
35: Plan files:
36: 
37: - You may create and edit plan Markdown files only.
38: - Follow the latest system reminder / Plan File section for the target plan location.
39: - Prefer `.kilo/plans/` with a concise kebab-case filename based on the plan details when no exact plan path is provided.
40: - Use `.plans/` or `.opencode/plans/` only when requested or required by the repo/client and your permissions allow it.
41: - Do not write the final plan or call `plan_exit` until the user chooses "Finalize and save the plan".
42: - After final approval, write the final plan to the chosen plan file, then call `plan_exit`. If `plan_exit` supports a path argument or the system reminder asks for one, pass the saved plan path.
43: - Do not edit source files or non-plan documentation files.
44: - Do not run mutating commands.
45: - If implementation requires source edits or mutating commands, tell the user to switch to an implementation-capable agent.
46: 
47: Completion behavior:
48: 
49: - Keep planning until the important design decisions are resolved or explicitly marked out of scope.
50: - If material uncertainty remains, keep the plan open: summarize the current state, identify the most important unresolved decision, and ask exactly one next question with your recommended answer.
51: - If the plan is implementation-ready but not saved, do not print the full plan in chat. Give a concise draft-ready summary, then ask exactly one question with these choices:
52:   1. Finalize and save the plan
53:   2. Continue refining
54: - Recommend "Finalize and save the plan" only when the goal, constraints, affected boundaries, data flow, failure modes, rollout or migration path, and validation plan are addressed or explicitly out of scope.
55: - If the user chooses "Finalize and save the plan", write the complete finalized Markdown plan to the chosen plan file, then call `plan_exit` as described above.
56: - If the user chooses "Continue refining", keep planning and do not write the final plan or call `plan_exit`.
57: - After `plan_exit`, rely on the client follow-up to ask whether the user wants to implement the saved plan in a new session.
58: - Do not implement source or documentation changes as this agent.
59: 
60: Saved plans should be concise and actionable. Prefer a clear ordered task list over a lengthy design document. Include only the context, decisions, risks, validation steps, and open questions another implementation-capable agent needs to execute safely.

(End of file - total 60 lines)
</content>

**Output:**
```
<skill_content name="webapp-testing">
# Skill: webapp-testing

# Web Application Testing

To test local web applications, write native Python Playwright scripts.

**Helper Scripts Available**:
- `scripts/with_server.py` - Manages server lifecycle (supports multiple servers)

**Always run scripts with `--help` first** to see usage. DO NOT read the source until you try running the script first and find that a customized solution is abslutely necessary. These scripts can be very large and thus pollute your context window. They exist to be called directly as black-box scripts rather than ingested into your context window.

## Decision Tree: Choosing Your Approach

```
User task → Is it static HTML?
    ├─ Yes → Read HTML file directly to identify selectors
    │         ├─ Success → Write Playwright script using selectors
    │         └─ Fails/Incomplete → Treat as dynamic (below)
    │
    └─ No (dynamic webapp) → Is the server already running?
        ├─ No → Run: python scripts/with_server.py --help
        │        Then use the helper + write simplified Playwright script
        │
        └─ Yes → Reconnaissance-then-action:
            1. Navigate and wait for networkidle
            2. Take screenshot or inspect DOM
            3. Identify selectors from rendered state
            4. Execute actions with discovered selectors
```

## Example: Using with_server.py

To start a server, run `--help` first, then use the helper:

**Single server:**
```bash
python scripts/with_server.py --server "npm run dev" --port 5173 -- python your_automation.py
```

**Multiple servers (e.g., backend + frontend):**
```bash
python scripts/with_server.py \
  --server "cd backend && python server.py" --port 3000 \
  --server "cd frontend && npm run dev" --port 5173 \
  -- python your_automation.py
```

To create an automation script, include only Playwright logic (servers are managed automatically):
```python
from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

with sync_playwright() as p:
    browser = p.chromium.launch(headless=True) # Always launch chromium in headless mode
    page = browser.new_page()
    page.goto('http://localhost:5173') # Server already running and ready
    page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle') # CRITICAL: Wait for JS to execute
    # ... your automation logic
    browser.close()
```

## Reconnaissance-Then-Action Pattern

1. **Inspect rendered DOM**:
   ```python
   page.screenshot(path='/tmp/inspect.png', full_page=True)
   content = page.content()
   page.locator('button').all()
   ```

2. **Identify selectors** from inspection results

3. **Execute actions** using discovered selectors

## Common Pitfall

❌ **Don't** inspect the DOM before waiting for `networkidle` on dynamic apps
✅ **Do** wait for `page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')` before inspection

## Best Practices

- **Use bundled scripts as black boxes** - To accomplish a task, consider whether one of the scripts available in `scripts/` can help. These scripts handle common, complex workflows reliably without cluttering the context window. Use `--help` to see usage, then invoke directly.
- Use `sync_playwright()` for synchronous scripts
- Always close the browser when done
- Use descriptive selectors: `text=`, `role=`, CSS selectors, or IDs
- Add appropriate waits: `page.wait_for_selector()` or `page.wait_for_timeout()`

## Reference Files

- **examples/** - Examples showing common patterns:
  - `element_discovery.py` - Discovering buttons, links, and inputs on a page
  - `static_html_automation.py` - Using file:// URLs for local HTML
  - `console_logging.py` - Capturing console logs during automation

Base directory for this skill: file:///home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing
Relative paths in this skill (e.g., scripts/, reference/) are relative to this base directory.
Note: file list is sampled.

<skill_files>
<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/LICENSE</file>
<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/examples/element_discovery.py</file>
<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/examples/console_logging.py</file>
<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/examples/static_html_automation.py</file>
<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/scripts/with_server.py</file>
<file>/home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/LICENSE.txt</file>
</skill_files>
</skill_content>
```

Test 1: Home page\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            title = page.title()\n            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()\n            search_input = page.locator('input[name=\"search\"]').count()\n            assert 'Beer Road Save' in h1 or 'Beer Road Save' in title, f\"Expected 'Beer Road Save' in h1='{h1}' or title='{title}'\"\n            assert search_input > 0, \"Search input not found\"\n            results.append(('Home page loads', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Home page loads', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 2: Breweries page\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/breweries')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()\n            selects = page.locator('select').count()\n            assert 'Breweries' in h1, f\"Expected 'Breweries' in h1='{h1}'\"\n            assert selects >= 4, f\"Expected >=4 selects, got {selects}\"\n            results.append(('Breweries page filter bar', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Breweries page filter bar', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 3: Login page\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/login')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()\n            email = page.locator('input[type=\"email\"]').count()\n            password = page.locator('input[type=\"password\"]').count()\n            assert 'Welcome back' in h1, f\"Expected 'Welcome back' in h1='{h1}'\"\n            assert email > 0, \"Email input not found\"\n            assert password > 0, \"Password input not found\"\n            results.append(('Login page form', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Login page form', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 4: Register page\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/register')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()\n            email = page.locator('input[type=\"email\"]').count()\n            assert 'Create an account' in h1, f\"Expected 'Create an account' in h1='{h1}'\"\n            assert email > 0, \"Email input not found\"\n            results.append(('Register page form', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Register page form', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 5: Trips redirect\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/trips')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            url = page.url\n            assert 'login' in url.lower(), f\"Expected redirect to login, got url='{url}'\"\n            results.append(('Trips page redirects', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Trips page redirects', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 6: 404 page\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/this-page-does-not-exist')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()\n            h2 = page.locator('h2').first.text_content()\n            assert '404' in h1, f\"Expected '404' in h1='{h1}'\"\n            assert 'Page Not Found' in h2, f\"Expected 'Page Not Found' in h2='{h2}'\"\n            results.append(('404 page renders', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('404 page renders', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 7: Challenges page\n        try:\n            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/challenges')\n            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')\n            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()\n            roulette = page.locator('text=🎰 Challenge Roulette').count()\n            assert 'Challenges' in h1, f\"Expected 'Challenges' in h1='{h1}'\"\n            assert roulette > 0, \"Challenge Roulette not found\"\n            results.append(('Challenges page loads', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Challenges page loads', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 8: Beers API\n        try:\n            response = page.request.get('http://localhost:5173/api/beers')\n            assert response.ok(), f\"Beers API returned {response.status()}\"\n            json = response.json()\n            assert 'data' in json, \"Missing 'data' in response\"\n            assert json.get('total', 0) > 0, \"Expected >0 beers\"\n            results.append(('Beers API returns data', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Beers API returns data', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        # Test 9: Challenges API\n        try:\n            response = page.request.get('http://localhost:5173/api/challenges')\n            assert response.ok(), f\"Challenges API returned {response.status()}\"\n            json = response.json()\n            assert 'data' in json, \"Missing 'data' in response\"\n            assert json.get('total', 0) > 0, \"Expected >0 challenges\"\n            results.append(('Challenges API returns data', 'PASS'))\n        except Exception as e:\n            results.append(('Challenges API returns data', f'FAIL: {e}'))\n        \n        browser.close()\n    \n    return results\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    print(\"=\" * 60)\n    print(\"Beer Road Save - Playwright Test Results\")\n    print(\"=\" * 60)\n    results = run_tests()\n    passed = sum(1 for _, r in results if r == 'PASS')\n    failed = sum(1 for _, r in results if r != 'PASS')\n    \n    for name, result in results:\n        status = \"✅\" if result == 'PASS' else \"❌\"\n        print(f\"{status} {name}: {result}\")\n    \n    print(\"=\" * 60)\n    print(f\"Total: {len(results)} | Passed: {passed} | Failed: {failed}\")\n    print(\"=\" * 60)\n    \n    if failed > 0:\n        exit(1)\n```\n\n**Step 4: Run the test script**\n\nFirst, make sure the dev server is running. Since we can't use npm/npx directly, we need to check if the server is already running:\n\nRun: `curl -s http://localhost:5173/ | head -20`\n\nIf the server is NOT running, we need to start it. They chose "webapp-testing skill" but I couldn't execute it. Then they said "continue" and I tried again but still couldn't execute shell commands.

I cannot execute shell commands in this environment, so I can't run the Playwright tests directly. However, I can give you the exact commands to run them yourself.

### Quick Start: Run Playwright Tests

```bash
# 1. Check if Playwright Python package is installed
python3 -c "import playwright; print('playwright version:', playwright.__version__)" 2>&1 || echo "PLAYWRIGHT_NOT_INSTALLED"

# 2. If not installed, install it
pip3 install playwright

# 3. Install Chromium browser
python3 -m playwright install chromium

# 4. Create the test script at: /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/tests/playwright_test.py
#    (use the script content I provided in the previous message)

# 5. Check if dev server is already running
curl -s http://localhost:5173/ | head -20

# 6. If server not running, start it in background
node /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/node_modules/vite/bin/vite.js --port 5173 --host 0.0.0.0 > /tmp/vite.log 2>&1 &
sleep 5

# 7. Run the tests
python3 /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/tests/playwright_test.py
```

### What the tests cover

The Python Playwright script tests 9 scenarios:
1. **Home page** — hero loads, search input visible
2. **Breweries page** — filter bar with ≥4 dropdowns
3. **Login page** — email/password form
4. **Register page** — registration form
5. **Trips redirect** — redirects to login when not authenticated
6. **404 page** — custom not-found page renders
7. **Challenges page** — roulette component loads
8. **Beers API** — returns data with `total > 0`
9. **Challenges API** — returns data with `total > 0`

### Alternative: Run via `with_server.py`

If you prefer the `webapp-testing` skill's server management helper:

```bash
python3 /home/anton/.agents/skills/webapp-testing/scripts/with_server.py \
  --server "node /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/node_modules/vite/bin/vite.js --port 5173" \
  --port 5173 \
  -- python3 /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/tests/playwright_test.py
```

This will auto-start the dev server, run the tests, and clean up afterward.

### Complete Test Execution Guide

**Step 1: Install Playwright Python package**
```bash
pip3 install playwright
```

**Step 2: Install Chromium browser**
```bash
python3 -m playwright install chromium
```

**Step 3: Create the test script**

Create file `/home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/tests/playwright_test.py` with this content:

```python
from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

def run_tests():
    results = []
    
    with sync_playwright() as p:
        browser = p.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        page = browser.new_page()
        
        # Test 1: Home page
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            title = page.title()
            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()
            search_input = page.locator('input[name="search"]').count()
            assert 'Beer Road Save' in h1 or 'Beer Road Save' in title, f"Expected 'Beer Road Save' in h1='{h1}' or title='{title}'"
            assert search_input > 0, "Search input not found"
            results.append(('Home page loads', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Home page loads', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 2: Breweries page
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/breweries')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()
            selects = page.locator('select').count()
            assert 'Breweries' in h1, f"Expected 'Breweries' in h1='{h1}'"
            assert selects >= 4, f"Expected >=4 selects, got {selects}"
            results.append(('Breweries page filter bar', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Breweries page filter bar', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 3: Login page
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/login')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()
            email = page.locator('input[type="email"]').count()
            password = page.locator('input[type="password"]').count()
            assert 'Welcome back' in h1, f"Expected 'Welcome back' in h1='{h1}'"
            assert email > 0, "Email input not found"
            assert password > 0, "Password input not found"
            results.append(('Login page form', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Login page form', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 4: Register page
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/register')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()
            email = page.locator('input[type="email"]').count()
            assert 'Create an account' in h1, f"Expected 'Create an account' in h1='{h1}'"
            assert email > 0, "Email input not found"
            results.append(('Register page form', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Register page form', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 5: Trips redirect
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/trips')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            url = page.url
            assert 'login' in url.lower(), f"Expected redirect to login, got url='{url}'"
            results.append(('Trips page redirects', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Trips page redirects', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 6: 404 page
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/this-page-does-not-exist')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()
            h2 = page.locator('h2').first.text_content()
            assert '404' in h1, f"Expected '404' in h1='{h1}'"
            assert 'Page Not Found' in h2, f"Expected 'Page Not Found' in h2='{h2}'"
            results.append(('404 page renders', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('404 page renders', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 7: Challenges page
        try:
            page.goto('http://localhost:5173/challenges')
            page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')
            h1 = page.locator('h1').first.text_content()
            roulette = page.locator('text=🎰 Challenge Roulette').count()
            assert 'Challenges' in h1, f"Expected 'Challenges' in h1='{h1}'"
            assert roulette > 0, "Challenge Roulette not found"
            results.append(('Challenges page loads', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Challenges page loads', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 8: Beers API
        try:
            response = page.request.get('http://localhost:5173/api/beers')
            assert response.ok(), f"Beers API returned {response.status()}"
            json = response.json()
            assert 'data' in json, "Missing 'data' in response"
            assert json.get('total', 0) > 0, "Expected >0 beers"
            results.append(('Beers API returns data', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Beers API returns data', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        # Test 9: Challenges API
        try:
            response = page.request.get('http://localhost:5173/api/challenges')
            assert response.ok(), f"Challenges API returned {response.status()}"
            json = response.json()
            assert 'data' in json, "Missing 'data' in response"
            assert json.get('total', 0) > 0, "Expected >0 challenges"
            results.append(('Challenges API returns data', 'PASS'))
        except Exception as e:
            results.append(('Challenges API returns data', f'FAIL: {e}'))
        
        browser.close()
    
    return results

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print("=" * 60)
    print("Beer Road Save - Playwright Test Results")
    print("=" * 60)
    results = run_tests()
    passed = sum(1 for _, r in results if r == 'PASS')
    failed = sum(1 for _, r in results if r != 'PASS')
    
    for name, result in results:
        status = "PASS" if result == 'PASS' else "FAIL"
        print(f"[{status}] {name}: {result}")
    
    print("=" * 60)
    print(f"Total: {len(results)} | Passed: {passed} | Failed: {failed}")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    if failed > 0:
        exit(1)
```

**Step 4: Ensure the dev server is running**

In a separate terminal, from `/home/anton/projects/beer-road`:
```bash
npm run dev
```

Or if you want to run just the frontend:
```bash
cd /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client
node node_modules/vite/bin/vite.js --port 5173
```

**Step 5: Run the tests**
```bash
python3 /home/anton/projects/beer-road/client/tests/playwright_test.py
```

342:                 <div>

344:                   <select
345:                     value={reviewForm.rating}
346:                     onChange={(e) => setReviewForm({ ...reviewForm, rating: parseInt(e.target.value) })}
347:                     className="w-full px-3 py-2.5 rounded-lg border border-gray-200 focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-primary-500"
348:                   >
349:                     {[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].map((n) => (
350:                       <option key={n} value={n}>{n} Star{n > 1 ? 's' : ''}</option>
351:                     ))}
352:                   </select>
353:                 </div>
354:                 <div>
355:                   <label className="block text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 mb-1.5">Comment</label>
356:                   <textarea
357:                     value={reviewForm.comment}
358:                     onChange={(e) => setReviewForm({ ...reviewForm, comment: e.target.value })}
359:                     rows={4}
360:                     className="w-full px-4 py-2.5 rounded-lg border border-gray-200 focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-primary-500 resize-none"
361:                     placeholder="Share your experience..."
362:                   />
363:                 </div>
364:                 <button
365:                   type="submit"
366:                   disabled={submittingReview}
367:                   className="w-full bg-primary-500 hover:bg-primary-600 disabled:bg-primary-400 text-white font-medium px-4 py-2.5 rounded-lg transition-colors"
368:                 >
369:                   {submittingReview ? 'Submitting...' : 'Submit Review'}
370:                 </button>
371:               </form>
372:             </div>
373:           )}
374:         </div>
375:       </div>
376:     </div>
377:   );
378: }
379: 
380: function BeersSection({ breweryId }) {
381:   const [beers, setBeers] = useState([]);
382:   const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
383: 
384:   useEffect(() => {
385:     const fetchBeers = async () => {
386:       try {
387:         const res = await fetch(`/api/beers/brewery/${breweryId}`);
388:         if (res.ok) {
389:           const data = await res.json();
390:           setBeers(data.data || []);
391:         }
392:       } catch (e) {
393:         console.error('Failed to fetch beers:', e);
394:       } finally {
395:         setLoading(false);
396:       }
397:     };
398:     fetchBeers();
399:   }, [breweryId]);
400: 
401:   if (loading) {
402:     return (
403:       <div className="mb-8">
404:         <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers</h2>
405:         <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
406:           {[1, 2, 3].map((i) => (
407:             <div key={i} className="bg-gray-50 rounded-xl p-4 animate-pulse">
408:               <div className="h-5 bg-gray-200 rounded mb-2" />
409:               <div className="h-4 bg-gray-200 rounded w-2/3" />
410:             </div>
411:           ))}
412:         </div>
413:       </div>
414:     );
415:   }
416: 
417:   if (beers.length === 0) {
418:     return (
419:       <div className="mb-8">
420:         <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers</h2>
421:         <p className="text-gray-500">No beers listed for this brewery yet.</p>
422:       </div>
423:     );
424:   }
425: 
426:   return (
427:     <div className="mb-8">
428:       <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers ({beers.length})</h2>
429:       <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
430:         {beers.map((beer) => (
431:           <Link
432:             key={beer.id}
433:             to={`/beers/${beer.id}`}
434:             className="bg-gray-50 rounded-xl p-4 hover:bg-gray-100 transition-colors"
435:           >
436:             <div className="flex items-start justify-between mb-2">
437:               <h3 className="font-semibold text-gray-900">{beer.name}</h3>
438:               <span className="text-2xl">🍺</span>
439:             </div>
440:             {beer.style && (
441:               <span className="inline-block px-2 py-0.5 bg-amber-100 text-amber-800 rounded-full text-xs font-medium mb-2">
442:                 {beer.style}
443:               </span>
444:             )}
445:             <div className="flex items-center gap-3 text-sm text-gray-600">
446:               {beer.abv && <span>ABV: {beer.abv}%</span>}
447:               {beer.ibu && <span>IBU: {beer.ibu}</span>}
448:             </div>
449:           </Link>
450:         ))}
451:       </div>
452:     </div>
453:   );
454: }

9: /**
10:  * Build a parameterized WHERE clause from the provided filters.
11:  * Returns { clause, params } where clause is "" or "WHERE ...".
12:  */
13: function buildBreweryFilters({ city, state, type, country, q }) {
14:   const conditions = [];
15:   const params = [];
16: 
17:   if (city) {
18:     conditions.push('city LIKE ?');
19:     params.push(`%${city}%`);
20:   }
21:   if (state) {
22:     conditions.push('state LIKE ?');
23:     params.push(`%${state}%`);
24:   }
25:   if (type) {
26:     conditions.push('type = ?');
27:     params.push(type);
28:   }
29:   if (country) {
30:     conditions.push('country = ?');
31:     params.push(country);
32:   }
33:   if (q) {
34:     // Free-text search across the most useful columns.
35:     conditions.push('(name LIKE ? OR description LIKE ? OR city LIKE ? OR address LIKE ?)');
36:     params.push(`%${q}%`, `%${q}%`, `%${q}%`, `%${q}%`);
37:   }
38: 
39:   const clause = conditions.length ? `WHERE ${conditions.join(' AND ')}` : '';
40:   return { clause, params };
41: }

220:     });

11: // WAL gives better concurrency for reads while writes happen.

13: // Enforce FK constraints so deletes/inserts stay consistent.
14: db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON');
15: 
16: /**
17:  * Create the schema (idempotent) and seed sample data on first run.
18:  * Called once at startup before the HTTP server starts accepting traffic.
19:  */
20: function initializeDatabase() {
21:   const schema = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'schema.sql'), 'utf-8');
22:   db.exec(schema);
23:   seedIfEmpty();
24:   return db;
25: }
26: 
27: /**
28:  * Insert seed breweries only when the breweries table is empty, so the
29:  * database can be safely re-initialized without duplicating rows.
30:  */
31: function seedIfEmpty() {
32:   const { count: breweries } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM breweries').get();
33:   const { count: beers } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM beers').get();
34:   if (breweries > 0 && beers > 0) return;
35: 
36:   const seed = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'seed.sql'), 'utf-8');
37:   // Wrap in a transaction so a partial failure leaves the DB untouched.
38:   // INSERT OR IGNORE keeps re-seeding idempotent (rows are no-ops if present).
39:   const tx = db.transaction(() => db.exec(seed));
40:   tx();
41: }
42: 
43: module.exports = { db, initializeDatabase, DB_PATH };

(End of file - total 43 lines)
</content>
```

const handleSpin = async () => {

    try {
      const res = await fetch('/api/challenges/random');
      if (res.ok) {
        const data = await res.json();
        setSpinning(false);
        await handleAccept(data.challenge.id);
      }
    } catch (e) {
      setSpinning(false);
      toast.error('Failed to get random challenge');
    }
  };
```

If `handleAccept` fails, `spinning` is set to false in the catch block, but what if it doesn't fail? After `setSpinning(false)`, `handleAccept` is called which might throw an error. If it throws, the catch block sets `spinning` to false again, which is fine. But wait - if `handleAccept` throws, the catch block handles it.

Actually, let me look at `handleAccept`:
```jsx
const handleAccept = async (challengeId) => {
    try {
      const res = await fetch(`/api/challenges/${challengeId}/accept`, {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      });
      if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Failed to accept challenge');
      toast.success('Challenge accepted!');
      fetchMyChallenges();
    } catch (err) {
      toast.error(err.message || 'Failed to accept challenge');
    }
  };
```

`handleAccept` catches its own errors and shows a toast. But it doesn't rethrow. So if `handleAccept` fails, `handleSpin` continues without setting `spinning` back to true. But the catch block in `handleSpin` wouldn't catch it because `handleAccept` already caught it.

So the flow is:
1. `setSpinning(true)` - button disabled, roulette starts animating
2. API call to `/api/challenges/random` succeeds
3. `setSpinning(false)` - button enabled, but...
4. `handleAccept` is called - if this succeeds, everything is fine. If it fails, it shows an error toast but doesn't set `spinning` back to false (it already is false from step 3).
5. The roulette animation completes and shows a selected challenge

17:     }
18:     return config;
19:   },
20:   (error) => Promise.reject(error)
21: );
22: 
23: api.interceptors.response.use(
24:   (response) => response,
25:   (error) => {
26:     if (error.response?.status === 401) {
27:       localStorage.removeItem('token');
28:       localStorage.removeItem('user');
29:       delete api.defaults.headers.common['Authorization'];
30:       window.location.href = '/login';
31:     }
32:     return Promise.reject(error);
33:   }
34: );
35: 
36: export default api;

(End of file - total 36 lines)
</content>
```

const handleSpin = () => {
  if (challenges.length === 0) {
    toast.error('No challenges available');
    return;
  }
  onSpin();  // This calls parent's handleSpin which does API call
  let counter = 0;
  const interval = setInterval(() => {
    const randomIndex = Math.floor(Math.random() * challenges.length);
    setSelectedChallenge(challenges[randomIndex]);
    counter++;
    if (counter > 20) {
      clearInterval(interval);
      const finalIndex = Math.floor(Math.random() * challenges.length);
      setSelectedChallenge(challenges[finalIndex]);
    }
  }, 100);
};
```

      setSpinning(false);
      await handleAccept(data.challenge.id);

    setSpinning(false);

  }
};

};

      await handleAccept(data.challenge.id);
    }
  } catch (e) {
    setSpinning(false);

  }
};
```

const handleAccept = async (challengeId) => {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/challenges/${challengeId}/accept`, {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
    });
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Failed to accept challenge');

};

Wait, let me check. In `AuthContext.jsx`:
```jsx
api.defaults.headers.common['Authorization'] = `Bearer ${token}`;
```

The `api` instance has the auth header. But `handleAccept` uses `fetch()` directly, not `api.post()`. So the Authorization header is NOT included!

};

```jsx
api.interceptors.response.use(
  (response) => response,
  (error) => {
    if (error.response?.status === 401) {
      localStorage.removeItem('token');
      localStorage.removeItem('user');
      delete api.defaults.headers.common['Authorization'];
      window.location.href = '/login';
    }
    return Promise.reject(error);
  }
);
```

So if the request returns 401, the interceptor redirects to login and rejects the promise. If the request returns 400 (e.g., already accepted), the promise is rejected without redirect.

Now let me identify the exact issues:

1. **Animation timing bug**: `onSpin()` is called immediately at the start of `handleSpin`, while the animation runs independently for 2 seconds. If the API responds before the animation ends, the result card shows but the animation keeps changing `selectedChallenge`. If the API responds after the animation, the result card shows the animation's challenge, not the API's actual challenge.

2. **Auth bug**: `handleAccept` and `fetchMyChallenges` in `Challenges.jsx` use raw `fetch()` instead of the authenticated `api` instance. The `api` instance automatically adds the JWT token via interceptors. Without the token, the `/accept` and `/my-challenges` endpoints return 401.

3. **Result card shows animation's random challenge, not the actual accepted challenge**: The roulette shows whatever the animation landed on, but the actual accepted challenge comes from the API response.

Actually, let me reconsider the `ChallengeRoulette` changes. If I move `onSpin()` to after the animation, and the parent uses `api.get('/api/challenges/random')`, the flow is:

But wait, there's a subtle issue. During the animation, `isAnimating` is true, so the button is disabled. But the button text says "Spin for Challenge" because `spinning` is false. After animation ends, `onSpin()` is called, parent sets `spinning = true`, button text changes to "Spinning...". After API responds, `spinning` becomes false, button text goes back to "Spin for Challenge".

This means the button text changes from "Spin for Challenge" → "Spinning..." → "Spin for Challenge". That's fine.

But actually, the user experience is:
1. Click "Spin for Challenge"
2. Animation runs for 2 seconds, button is disabled
3. After animation, button text changes to "Spinning..."
4. After API responds (usually fast), button text goes back to "Spin for Challenge"
5. Result card shows

The "Spinning..." state might be very brief (local SQLite query is fast). That's acceptable.

If `handleAccept` succeeds, `spinning` is already false, and the result card shows.
If `handleAccept` fails, `spinning` is already false, and the result card shows but the challenge wasn't actually accepted.

This is acceptable behavior. The user sees the challenge and a success toast, or an error toast.

I've found two bugs causing the roulette spin to fail:

**Bug 1: Missing auth token on challenge acceptance** — `handleAccept` and `fetchMyChallenges` in `Challenges.jsx` use raw `fetch()` instead of the authenticated `api` instance, so the JWT is never sent. The `/accept` and `/my-challenges` endpoints return 401.

**Bug 2: Animation/API race condition** — `ChallengeRoulette` calls `onSpin()` immediately while the animation runs independently for 2 seconds. If the API responds before the animation ends, the result card shows but keeps changing. If after, it shows the animation's random pick instead of the API's actual challenge.

**ChallengeRoulette.jsx** — Rewritten so the 2-second local animation runs first, then `onSpin()` is called only after it completes. The result card now uses `resultChallenge || selectedChallenge` and only renders when both `spinning` and `isAnimating` are false.

**Challenges.jsx** — Seven edits applied:
- `import api from '../utils/api'` added (line 6)
- `resultChallenge` state added (line 14)
- `fetchMyChallenges` now uses `api.get` instead of raw `fetch` (line 37)
- `handleAccept` now uses `api.post` instead of raw `fetch` (line 46)
- `handleSpin` now uses `api.get('/api/challenges/random')`, stores result in `resultChallenge`, then calls `handleAccept` (line 56)
- `ChallengeRoulette` props updated to pass `resultChallenge` (line 85)
- `CompleteChallengeButton.handleSubmit` now uses `api.post` instead of raw `fetch` (line 157)
</task_result>
</task>

    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('Brewery not found');

    setLoading(false);

};

```js
router.get('/:id', (req, res, next) => {
  try {
    const brewery = db
      .prepare('SELECT * FROM breweries WHERE id = ?')
      .get(req.params.id);
    if (!brewery) {
      const err = new Error('Brewery not found');
      err.status = 404;
      throw err;
    }
    res.json({ brewery });
  } catch (err) {
    next(err);
  }
});
```

router.get('/:id', (req, res, next) => {
  try {
    const brewery = db
      .prepare('SELECT * FROM breweries WHERE id = ?')
      .get(req.params.id);
    if (!brewery) {
      const err = new Error('Brewery not found');
      err.status = 404;
      throw err;
    }
    res.json({ brewery });
  } catch (err) {
    next(err);
  }
});
```

If any of these throw an uncaught error, React might unmount the component. But they all have try/catch blocks.

Wait, the third useEffect:
```jsx
useEffect(() => {
  const checkFavorite = async () => {
    try {
      const res = await fetch(`/api/favorites/${id}`);
      setIsFavorite(res.ok);
    } catch (e) {
      setIsFavorite(false);
    }
  };
  const fetchUserTrips = async () => {
    try {
      const res = await fetch('/api/trips');
      if (res.ok) {
        const data = await res.json();
        setUserTrips(data.trips || data);
      }
    } catch (e) {
      console.error('Failed to fetch trips:', e);
    }
  };
  if (user) {
    checkFavorite();
    fetchUserTrips();
  }
}, [id, user]);
```

This uses raw `fetch` too. If the user is not logged in, `user` is null, so neither function is called. This should be fine.

Actually, wait. Looking at `fetchUserTrips`:

If the user is NOT logged in, the `/api/trips` endpoint might return 401. But since `user` is null, this code doesn't run.

131:           </>

Actually, let me re-examine the backend `breweries.js` route for `/:id`:
```javascript
router.get('/:id', (req, res, next) => {
  try {
    const brewery = db
      .prepare('SELECT * FROM breweries WHERE id = ?')
      .get(req.params.id);
    if (!brewery) {
      const err = new Error('Brewery not found');
      err.status = 404;
      throw err;
    }
    res.json({ brewery });
  } catch (err) {
    next(err);
  }
});
```

This looks correct. The only way it would fail is if:
1. `req.params.id` doesn't match any brewery (but 13 exists in seed)
2. Database connection fails
3. JSON serialization fails (unlikely)

Actually, I just thought of something: What if the `id` parameter is being interpreted as a string and SQLite is doing a string comparison instead of integer comparison? In SQLite, `id` is `INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT`. When you do `WHERE id = ?` with a string parameter '13', SQLite should still match because it converts the string to a number for comparison. But let me verify this is not an issue...

Actually, in Better-SQLite3, parameters are bound as their native types. If `req.params.id` is a string "13", it would be bound as a string. SQLite's type system is dynamic, so `WHERE id = '13'` would match rows where `id = 13` because SQLite converts the string to a number for numeric comparison. So this shouldn't be an issue.

261:                   className="px-5 py-2.5 bg-primary-500 text-white rounded-xl font-medium hover:bg-primary-600 transition-colors"

295:           <BeersSection breweryId={brewery.id} />
296:         </div>

303:             <div className="space-y-4">

307:                   <div className="h-3 bg-gray-200 rounded w-3/4" />
308:                 </div>
309:               ))}
310:             </div>
311:           ) : reviews.length === 0 ? (
312:             <div className="bg-white rounded-xl border border-gray-100 p-8 text-center">

314:               <p className="text-gray-500">No reviews yet. Be the first to share your experience!</p>

316:           ) : (
317:             <div className="space-y-4">

322:                       {(review.userName || review.user?.name || 'U')[0].toUpperCase()}
323:                     </div>
324:                     <div>
325:                       <p className="font-semibold text-gray-900 text-sm">{review.userName || review.user?.name || 'User'}</p>
326:                       <ReviewStars rating={review.rating} />
327:                     </div>
328:                   </div>
329:                   {review.comment && <p className="text-gray-700 text-sm mt-2 leading-relaxed">{review.comment}</p>}
330:                   <p className="text-xs text-gray-400 mt-3">{new Date(review.createdAt).toLocaleDateString()}</p>
331:                 </div>
332:               ))}
333:             </div>
334:           )}
335:         </div>

338:           {user && (

341:               <form onSubmit={handleSubmitReview} className="space-y-4">
342:                 <div>
343:                   <label className="block text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 mb-1.5">Rating</label>
344:                   <select
345:                     value={reviewForm.rating}
346:                     onChange={(e) => setReviewForm({ ...reviewForm, rating: parseInt(e.target.value) })}
347:                     className="w-full px-3 py-2.5 rounded-lg border border-gray-200 focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-primary-500"
348:                   >
349:                     {[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].map((n) => (
350:                       <option key={n} value={n}>{n} Star{n > 1 ? 's' : ''}</option>

352:                   </select>
353:                 </div>
354:                 <div>
355:                   <label className="block text-sm font-medium text-gray-700 mb-1.5">Comment</label>

358:                     onChange={(e) => setReviewForm({ ...reviewForm, comment: e.target.value })}
359:                     rows={4}

361:                     placeholder="Share your experience..."
362:                   />
363:                 </div>
364:                 <button
365:                   type="submit"
366:                   disabled={submittingReview}
367:                   className="w-full bg-primary-500 hover:bg-primary-600 disabled:bg-primary-400 text-white font-medium px-4 py-2.5 rounded-lg transition-colors"
368:                 >
369:                   {submittingReview ? 'Submitting...' : 'Submit Review'}
370:                 </button>
371:               </form>
372:             </div>
373:           )}
374:         </div>
375:       </div>
376:     </div>
377:   );
378: }
379: 
380: function BeersSection({ breweryId }) {
381:   const [beers, setBeers] = useState([]);
382:   const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
383: 
384:   useEffect(() => {
385:     const fetchBeers = async () => {
386:       try {
387:         const res = await fetch(`/api/beers/brewery/${breweryId}`);
388:         if (res.ok) {
389:           const data = await res.json();
390:           setBeers(data.data || []);
391:         }
392:       } catch (e) {
393:         console.error('Failed to fetch beers:', e);
394:       } finally {
395:         setLoading(false);
396:       }
397:     };
398:     fetchBeers();
399:   }, [breweryId]);
400: 
401:   if (loading) {
402:     return (
403:       <div className="mb-8">
404:         <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers</h2>
405:         <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
406:           {[1, 2, 3].map((i) => (
407:             <div key={i} className="bg-gray-50 rounded-xl p-4 animate-pulse">
408:               <div className="h-5 bg-gray-200 rounded mb-2" />
409:               <div className="h-4 bg-gray-200 rounded w-2/3" />
410:             </div>
411:           ))}
412:         </div>
413:       </div>
414:     );
415:   }
416: 
417:   if (beers.length === 0) {
418:     return (
419:       <div className="mb-8">
420:         <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers</h2>
421:         <p className="text-gray-500">No beers listed for this brewery yet.</p>
422:       </div>
423:     );
424:   }
425: 
426:   return (
427:     <div className="mb-8">
428:       <h2 className="text-xl font-semibold text-gray-900 mb-4">Beers ({beers.length})</h2>
429:       <div className="grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3 gap-4">
430:         {beers.map((beer) => (
431:           <Link
432:             key={beer.id}
433:             to={`/beers/${beer.id}`}
434:             className="bg-gray-50 rounded-xl p-4 hover:bg-gray-100 transition-colors"
435:           >
436:             <div className="flex items-start justify-between mb-2">
437:               <h3 className="font-semibold text-gray-900">{beer.name}</h3>
438:               <span className="text-2xl">🍺</span>
439:             </div>
440:             {beer.style && (
441:               <span className="inline-block px-2 py-0.5 bg-amber-100 text-amber-800 rounded-full text-xs font-medium mb-2">
442:                 {beer.style}
443:               </span>
444:             )}
445:             <div className="flex items-center gap-3 text-sm text-gray-600">
446:               {beer.abv && <span>ABV: {beer.abv}%</span>}
447:               {beer.ibu && <span>IBU: {beer.ibu}</span>}
448:             </div>
449:           </Link>
450:         ))}
451:       </div>
452:     </div>
453:   );
454: }

(End of file - total 454 lines)
</content>
```

function seedIfEmpty() {
  const { count: breweries } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM breweries').get();
  const { count: beers } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM beers').get();

  // ... seed
}
```

  const schema = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'schema.sql'), 'utf-8');
  db.exec(schema);
  seedIfEmpty();
  return db;
}
```

And `schema.sql` has `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, so it's safe to run multiple times. And `seedIfEmpty` only seeds if both tables are empty.

}
```

        localStorage.removeItem('user');
        localStorage.removeItem('token');
      }
    }
    setLoading(false);
  }, []);
```

api.interceptors.response.use(

);

```javascript
function seedIfEmpty() {
  const { count: breweries } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM breweries').get();
  const { count: beers } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM beers').get();
  if (breweries > 0 && beers > 0) return;

  const seed = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'seed.sql'), 'utf-8');
  const tx = db.transaction(() => db.exec(seed));
  tx();
}
```

The seed only runs if BOTH tables are empty. If the database already exists and has some data (e.g., from a previous run), the seed won't run. But if the database is fresh, both tables would be empty and the seed would run.

  const seed = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'seed.sql'), 'utf-8');
  const tx = db.transaction(() => db.exec(seed));
  tx();
}
```

It only seeds if BOTH tables are empty. If the database already exists and has breweries but no beers, the seed won't run. But the seed inserts both breweries and beers.

But wait - what if the database was created and the seed ran, but then the database was deleted or corrupted? Or what if the seed didn't run because the tables already existed but were empty?

Looking at `seedIfEmpty()`:
```javascript
function seedIfEmpty() {
  const { count: breweries } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM breweries').get();
  const { count: beers } = db.prepare('SELECT COUNT(*) AS count FROM beers').get();
  if (breweries > 0 && beers > 0) return;

  const seed = fs.readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, 'seed.sql'), 'utf-8');
  const tx = db.transaction(() => db.exec(seed));
  tx();
}
```

If the tables exist but are empty, `breweries` would be 0 and `beers` would be 0, so the seed would run.

If the tables don't exist, `initializeDatabase()` runs the schema first, which creates the tables. Then `seedIfEmpty()` runs, and since the tables are empty, the seed runs.

So the seed should always run on a fresh database.

But what if the database file exists but is corrupted? Then `initializeDatabase()` might fail.

In development with Vite, the dev server proxies `/api` to `http://localhost:3001`. So `/api/breweries/13` should be proxied correctly.

But what if the proxy is not working? Then the fetch would go to `http://localhost:5173/api/breweries/13` (the Vite dev server), which doesn't have that route. The Vite dev server would return 404, and the frontend would show "Brewery not found".

In the `BreweryDetail` component:

const res = await fetch(`/api/breweries/${id}`);

If the fetch fails with a network error, `res` might be undefined or the error would be thrown before `res.ok` is checked. Actually, `fetch` only rejects on network errors, not on HTTP errors. So if the proxy is working but the backend returns 404, `res.ok` would be false and we'd throw "Brewery not found".

If the proxy is not working (backend not running), `fetch` would reject with a TypeError (network error). This would be caught by the catch block, and `error` would be set to something like "Failed to fetch" or "NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource."

In either case, the error state would show, not an empty page.

const DB_PATH = process.env.DB_PATH || path.join(__dirname, 'beer-road.db');
